question: A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic because of joint pain and a rash. He reports that he spent a weekend hiking in a wooded area in upstate New York two weeks ago and noticed a tick bite on his leg upon returning home. Since then, he has developed a mild fever, fatigue, and generalized body aches. The joint pain is migratory, affecting his knees, elbows, and wrists, and he has noticed a circular, red rash on his abdomen. He denies any significant past medical history and takes no medications regularly. On examination, there are no swollen joints or synovitis, but multiple erythematous macules resembling target lesions are noted. Laboratory tests reveal mild leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances for the past six months. He describes the pain as a constant dull ache affecting his neck, shoulders, back, and hips. The fatigue is pervasive, and he often wakes up feeling unrested despite a full night's sleep. He reports no recent illnesses or significant weight changes. The patient denies any morning stiffness lasting over 30 minutes. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple soft tissue sites but no joint swelling or deformities. Vital signs are within normal ranges. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 74-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe back pain. He reports that the pain started suddenly while he was bending over to lift a light object. His medical history includes chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and well-controlled hypertension. He denies any recent trauma or falls. On examination, there is localized tenderness over the thoracic spine. Neurological examination is unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal normal calcium levels and slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Imaging of the spine shows a compression fracture at the T8 vertebral level. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's spinal condition?

A 34-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with a 3-month history of a red rash over his cheeks and nose that worsens with sun exposure. He also reports joint pain in his hands and knees that is most severe in the morning and improves as the day progresses. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. He works as an accountant with no known exposure to industrial chemicals. Physical examination reveals a well-demarcated, erythematous rash on the malar area sparing the nasolabial folds. There is mild swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show positive antinuclear antibodies and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. He describes the pain as a deep, throbbing ache that is more pronounced in the evenings and after physical activity. The patient reports some morning stiffness, but it typically resolves within 30 minutes. He denies any significant trauma or recent infections. His medical history is notable for mild psoriasis, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, both hips have a limited range of motion with discomfort on internal rotation. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but his rheumatoid factor is negative. An X-ray of the hips reveals erosions and sclerosis at the sacroiliac joints. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent oral ulcers and painful sores on his genitals that have been occurring for the past 6 months. He also reports feeling fatigued and experiencing joint pain, mainly in his knees and ankles. The patient denies any significant past medical history and does not take any regular medications. He does not smoke or use recreational drugs, but drinks alcohol occasionally. On examination, multiple aphthous ulcers are noted in the mouth, and there are healing ulcers present on the genitals. There is no synovitis or noticeable joint swelling. Laboratory studies show a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, raised, red nodules on his shins and generalized joint pain for the past 2 weeks. He also reports fatigue and mild fever but denies any recent infections or new medications. He works as a software engineer and has no significant medical history. Physical examination reveals tender erythematous nodules on both lower legs and swelling in the knees and ankles. Laboratory tests show mild leukocytosis and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A chest X-ray is performed and demonstrates bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue, weight gain, and occasional joint stiffness over the past six months. He reports feeling tired despite getting adequate sleep and has noticed a slight swelling in his neck. He denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. On physical examination, the patient has a palpable, non-tender goiter. Laboratory tests reveal a low free T4 level with a high TSH. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic complaining of progressively worsening weakness in his shoulders and hips over the past several months. He denies any significant joint pain but mentions difficulty climbing stairs and lifting heavy objects. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. He has no history of recent infections or travel. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness in both the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels, while inflammatory markers are within normal range. Which is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic due to progressive fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past 6 months. He also reports generalized weakness and occasional joint pain. His medical history includes hypertension, but he is otherwise healthy and takes no medications except for a daily multivitamin. Physical examination reveals pallor and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 9.8 g/dL
Hematocrit: 29%
Mean corpuscular volume: 82 fL
White blood cell count: 6,500/mm^3
Platelet count: 150,000/mm^3
Serum
Iron: 25 µg/dL
Total iron binding capacity: 400 µg/dL
Ferritin: 15 ng/mL

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past few months. He has also noted feeling more lethargic and has experienced some constipation. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both of which are well controlled with medication. On examination, his temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 134/86 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum calcium levels. Further imaging of the bones shows diffuse osteopenia. The serum alkaline phosphatase level is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of nasal congestion, sinus pressure, and occasional nosebleeds. He also reports experiencing a persistent cough and shortness of breath. Over the last few weeks, he has noticed swelling in his legs and foamy urine. His medical history includes seasonal allergies. On examination, he has bilateral crackles on lung auscultation and mild edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated serum creatinine and urinalysis shows proteinuria. A chest X-ray displays multiple pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a complaint of widespread musculoskeletal pain that has been ongoing for the past six months. He reports feeling constantly fatigued and mentions that he has difficulty sleeping, often waking up feeling unrefreshed. He does not have morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, but he describes his muscles as feeling sore and tender. He denies any joint swelling. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, there are tender points in multiple areas including the neck, shoulders, and lower back. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, metabolic panel, and inflammatory markers including erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of unexplained weight gain and noticeable puffiness around his eyes. He also reports stiffness in his joints, particularly in the morning, which subsides as the day progresses. He denies any changes in diet or physical activity and has no known allergies. Past medical history is notable only for seasonal allergies. On physical examination, the patient has periorbital edema and slight swelling of the hands. Laboratory results reveal normal blood glucose levels, but urinalysis indicates proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of skin changes and finger discoloration over the past year. He reports that his fingers turn white then blue upon exposure to cold, followed by redness and pain as they warm. The patient also notes that the skin on his fingers has become tight and shiny, making it difficult to bend his fingers. He denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. His medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, the skin over his fingers is taut and there is pallor and cyanosis of the fingertips. Laboratory tests show the presence of anti-centromere antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male visits his primary care physician complaining of persistent bone pain and fatigue. Over the past two months, he reports increasing difficulty in climbing stairs and lifting objects due to weakness. There is no significant joint swelling, but he mentions a vague sense of joint discomfort. His medical history includes hypertension and he is a former smoker. Laboratory investigations reveal elevated calcium levels and low parathyroid hormone. Radiological imaging shows lytic lesions in the long bones. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue, joint pain, and a rash. He reports being bitten by a tick approximately three weeks ago while hiking in a wooded area. Since then, he has developed an expanding erythematous rash with central clearing on his left thigh, as well as arthralgia in multiple joints. The patient denies any previous history of joint disease. He does not recall any fever or chills. On physical examination, there is a large, erythematous, annular rash on the left thigh, and swelling in the knees and wrists. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) but normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, unintentional weight loss, and night sweats. He reports persistent swelling in his neck and axillae that has not improved and occasionally experiences mild discomfort in these areas. He also has occasional joint pain but denies any morning stiffness or joint deformities. He is a non-smoker and has no significant past medical history. Vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there are multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical and axillary lymph nodes. Laboratory studies reveal mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A biopsy of the cervical lymph node is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening pain in both hips, which has significantly impaired his ability to walk long distances. He reports mild morning stiffness that resolves after about 30 minutes. He denies any recent trauma or significant changes in weight. His medical history is notable for hyperlipidemia, and he is a former smoker. On examination, the hip joints are tender on palpation with reduced range of motion but show no visible swelling. Laboratory tests reveal normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. X-rays of the pelvis show subchondral sclerosis and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue, weight loss, and painful subcutaneous nodules over his elbows and forearms. He reports persistent low-grade fevers over the past month and generalized malaise. He denies any joint swelling or pain. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. On examination, the nodules are firm and located on extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and normocytic anemia. A biopsy of one of the nodules shows necrotizing vasculitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of worsening pain and swelling in his hands and wrists over the past six months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning, lasting for over an hour before improving somewhat during the day. He has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen for the pain with some relief. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and high levels of rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man visits his primary care physician because of unexplained weight gain over the past six months, accompanied by periorbital edema and joint stiffness, particularly in the morning. He also reports feeling fatigued and has had generalized weakness. He denies any recent changes in diet or physical activity and has no significant past medical history. On physical examination, he has moderate periorbital edema and slight puffiness of the face. His blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, and his heart rate is 78/min. Laboratory tests reveal normal thyroid function tests, a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, fever, and a rash. He mentions a recent history of hiking a few weeks ago in a wooded area and recalls removing a tick from his leg. On physical exam, the patient has an erythematous rash with central clearing on his thigh and swelling in the right knee. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild leukocytosis. Given the recent tick exposure and clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with progressive difficulty in walking over the past six months. He reports weakness in his legs, particularly after exertion, and occasional muscle cramps. He denies any numbness or tingling. His medical history is significant for well-managed diabetes mellitus. A recent electromyography (EMG) shows decreased nerve conduction velocities. On examination, there is bilateral lower extremity weakness with diminished deep tendon reflexes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back pain and stiffness. He reports the pain started insidiously over the past year, and it is worse in the morning, improving with activity as the day progresses. He denies any recent trauma or falls. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, and spinal flexibility is reduced. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. The patient mentions that he sometimes experiences eye redness and discomfort. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that developed over the past two weeks. He recently returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States, where he remembers noticing a tick bite on his leg. The rash initially appeared as a small red spot at the site of the bite but has since expanded into a large, circular, erythematous lesion with central clearing. He reports feeling fatigued and has been experiencing headaches and muscle aches. On examination, he has a mildly swollen and tender right knee and an annular rash on his left thigh. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue that began shortly after recovering from a viral upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. He denies any joint swelling but describes his pain as muscular and generalized. He has no significant past medical history and his family history is unremarkable. On physical examination, he exhibits tenderness over the muscles of his upper arms and thighs but no erythema or joint effusion. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe mid-back pain after attempting to lift a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and exacerbated with movement. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. His medications include lisinopril and metformin. Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation over the thoracic vertebrae with limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is normal. Laboratory studies show normal inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion and occasional nosebleeds. He reports a chronic cough and feels more tired than usual. Additionally, he notes difficulty breathing and blood in his urine over the past few weeks. He has a history of recurrent sinus infections and has been treated with several courses of antibiotics with no improvement. On physical examination, there are no joint deformities, but he has mild facial tenderness and bilateral crackles heard on lung auscultation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, and urinalysis shows hematuria with red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain in his knees and ankles. He reports feeling generally unwell with low-grade fevers and fatigue. He denies any recent travel, insect bites, or significant changes in diet. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On examination, there are tender, erythematous nodules over the anterior aspects of his lower legs, and swelling with tenderness in his knees and ankles. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, including an increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic due to progressive difficulty walking over the past 4 months. He reports weakness in his legs that worsens with activity and improves somewhat with rest. He also describes occasional numbness and tingling in his lower extremities. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, muscle strength is 4/5 in the lower extremities with diminished deep tendon reflexes. There is mild atrophy of the calf muscles. Laboratory studies show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past few months. He notes that the joint pain is primarily in his wrists and knees and is worse in the morning. He also reports that his skin seems to bruise with minimal trauma, and he feels more tired than usual despite getting adequate sleep. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, there is pallor of the conjunctivae and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 8 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,000/mm³, and platelets of 90,000/mm³. Bone marrow aspiration shows hypocellular marrow with increased fat spaces. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 34-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain. He reports profound fatigue and unrefreshing sleep, stating that he often feels more tired upon waking. His occupation as a software engineer involves long hours sitting at a desk, but he denies any recent injuries or changes in physical activity. He also describes frequent tension headaches and difficulty concentrating. Physical examination reveals diffuse tenderness in several areas, including the trapezius, supraspinatus, and gluteal regions, but no synovitis or joint swelling is noted. Laboratory workup shows normal inflammatory markers including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks following a viral illness that his family also experienced. He notes that his symptoms initially resembled a mild flu. He reports persistent muscle pain and joint aches, particularly in the wrists and ankles. He denies any significant swelling or stiffness in the morning. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On physical examination, there is mild tenderness in the wrists and ankles but no visible swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and difficulty concentrating over the past six months. He also reports weight gain and occasional muscle aches, primarily in his upper arms and thighs. He denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable except for high blood pressure, which is well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, his heart rate is 60/min, and blood pressure is 130/80 mm Hg. Laboratory studies reveal a serum TSH level of 8.5 mIU/L (reference range: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L) and free T4 of 0.7 ng/dL (reference range: 0.8-1.8 ng/dL). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening on his fingers and hands, which he has noticed over the past several months. He also describes episodes of color changes in his fingers, which turn white then blue in cold weather or when stressed, followed by redness upon rewarming. He reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. On examination, there is evidence of fibrosis on the skin of his fingers and decreased range of motion in his hands. Laboratory tests show positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and mild anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of a purplish discoloration on his legs that appears in a net-like pattern and has been associated with intermittent episodes of pain and swelling. He also reports recurrent episodes of leg swelling, especially after prolonged standing or sitting. His past medical history includes a deep vein thrombosis (DVT) two years ago. He is currently on anticoagulation therapy but admits to non-compliance at times. Laboratory tests reveal a mild thrombocytopenia and elevated inflammatory markers. On examination, livedo reticularis is observed on the lower extremities. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain and swelling after slipping on wet pavement and falling onto his outstretched hand. He has a history of occasional morning stiffness in his fingers that resolves within an hour and describes some intermittent swelling in his knees. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling over the anatomical snuffbox of the right wrist, with decreased range of motion due to pain. He denies any numbness or tingling in the hand. An x-ray of the wrist is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with sudden onset pain and swelling in multiple joints. He reports that the symptoms began a week ago, starting with his knees and now involving his ankles and wrists. He denies any prior history of joint problems and is not currently taking any medications. The patient recently returned from a trip to Southeast Asia where he was on a business trip for two weeks. He notes feeling fatigued with a mild fever since returning. On examination, the affected joints are tender and swollen, but there is no visible erythema. Laboratory results show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, a rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure, and painful oral ulcers. He reports that his symptoms have been intermittent over the past several months, with joint pain affecting his wrists and knees. He denies having any significant morning stiffness, but notes that his symptoms are exacerbated by sunlight. On examination, he has a maculopapular rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and shallow ulcers on the buccal mucosa. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and low complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and swelling. He notes that the pain is primarily in his fingers and toes and is asymmetric. He also reports patches of red, scaly skin on his elbows and knees, which have been present for several years. He denies any significant morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals dactylitis of the digits and silvery plaques on the extensor surfaces. Lab tests show negative rheumatoid factor and elevated acute phase reactants. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old male comes to the clinic with a history of joint pain that has been migrating between his knees, ankles, and elbows over the past three weeks. He reports that two weeks ago, he had a severe sore throat which resolved without treatment. On physical examination, his temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 92/min, and respirations are 18/min. There is noticeable swelling and tenderness in the affected joints. A throat culture was not performed at the time of his sore throat. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in his right arm and hand. He reports that these symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past six months. He works as a software developer and spends long hours at the computer. He denies any recent trauma or injury. Medical history is significant for mild hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome. Reflexes in the upper extremities are diminished. A Spurling's test performed on the right side reproduces the patient's symptoms. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 52-year-old man visits his primary care physician complaining of chronic pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past 18 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and mentions the development of small, firm nodules under the skin on his elbows. He has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are also detected. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He mentions that he had a severe cold with a fever and sore throat three weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Since then, he has noticed generalized muscle pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings, which improves slightly throughout the day. He reports no joint swelling or redness. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation of the muscles in the shoulders and thighs. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal creatine kinase and liver function tests. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Initiate treatment with nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)
options.B: Order an MRI of the affected areas

question: A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 6-month history of chronic neck pain and occasional tingling in his right arm. He describes the pain as dull and constant, with intermittent sharp exacerbations. The tingling often occurs when he turns his head to the right. He denies any recent trauma but mentions his job involves long hours at a desk. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the neck and diminished triceps reflex on the right. Spurling's maneuver reproduces his symptoms. An MRI of the cervical spine is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old male presents to the clinic with unexplained weight gain and swelling around his eyes for the past few weeks. He also reports morning stiffness in his joints that improves as the day progresses. The patient denies any changes in diet, activity level, or recent travel. He has a history of seasonal allergies but otherwise has been healthy. Physical examination reveals 1+ pitting edema in the lower extremities and periorbital edema. Laboratory tests show mild proteinuria and normal renal function. His anti-nuclear antibody test is negative, but ESR is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic with concerns about unexplained weight gain and swelling around his eyes. He reports feeling more fatigued and also experiencing stiffness in his fingers, especially in the morning, which improves as the day progresses. His medical history includes well-controlled hypertension. He mentions no recent changes in diet or physical activity levels. On examination, he has periorbital edema, mild puffiness in his hands, and normal vital signs. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, elevated serum creatinine, and mild proteinuria. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and unrefreshing sleep for the past several months. He describes a constant, dull ache that affects his neck, back, and arms. He has difficulty concentrating at work and feels tired despite sleeping for 8 hours each night. The patient denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. His medical history is significant for anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome. On physical examination, the patient appears well-nourished. Tenderness is noted upon palpation of several soft tissue areas, although no joint swelling or erythema is observed. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, metabolic panel, and thyroid function tests, are within normal limits. His erythrocyte sedimentation rate is slightly elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has gradually worsened over the past 6 months. He describes the pain as a deep aching sensation, exacerbated by prolonged sitting and standing. The patient also reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. He has a history of mild hypertension, controlled with medication, and has been experiencing fatigue. He denies any recent trauma or significant weight changes. On examination, there is tenderness over the hips with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) but are negative for rheumatoid factor. Radiographic imaging of the pelvis shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in the hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.F: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 60-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of chronic hand and wrist pain that has been progressively worsening over the past year. He reports stiffness in the morning that lasts for over an hour, along with swelling of the joints. He has difficulty with daily tasks such as opening jars and buttoning his shirt. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal joints bilaterally and several subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is positive. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has been persistent for the past two years. He reports that the pain is dull, worsens in the morning, and gradually improves with physical activity throughout the day. He also mentions feeling fatigued and experiencing pain in his hips and shoulders occasionally. He denies any history of trauma or significant previous back injuries. On physical examination, there is limited spinal mobility with a positive Schober's test. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care doctor with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past several months. He also notes occasional swelling of his face, particularly around the jaw area, which he finds concerning. He mentions being more tired than usual and has been experiencing joint pain, especially in his hands and wrists. On examination, bilateral parotid gland enlargement is observed. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein. Further workup reveals positive anti-SSA and anti-SSB antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue and general malaise for the past six months. He reports occasional joint pain and muscle weakness, but denies any morning stiffness. He has had difficulty concentrating at work and feels colder than usual, even in warm environments. On physical examination, he appears pale and has a slow heart rate of 58 bpm. Lab work shows the following:

**Thyroid Function Tests:**
- TSH: 8.0 mIU/L (normal: 0.4-4.0 mIU/L)
- Free T4: 0.5 ng/dL (normal: 0.8-2.0 ng/dL)

Routine blood tests show mild anemia, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of chronic fatigue and recent weight gain. He reports feeling cold more frequently and has noticed some swelling in his neck. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness. Physical examination reveals dry skin and a diffusely enlarged thyroid gland. Laboratory studies show elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of rash and joint pain. He reports purplish spots on his lower legs and occasional blood in his urine. The patient states that he has had joint pain in his knees and ankles, which is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. He denies any recent travel or new medications. His medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension, and he takes lisinopril. On examination, palpable purpura is noted on the lower extremities. Joint examination reveals tenderness without swelling. Urinalysis shows microscopic hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of skin tightening on his fingers and frequent episodes of color changes in his hands upon exposure to cold. He describes the episodes starting with whitening of his fingers, followed by a bluish color, and then redness upon rewarming. He reports occasional joint pain and stiffness in his hands, which lasts less than 30 minutes in the morning. He denies any significant fatigue or systemic symptoms. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, there is thickening and tightening of the skin over the fingers (sclerodactyly), and digital ulcers are noted. There is no synovitis or joint swelling. Laboratory tests reveal a negative rheumatoid factor and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 62-year-old man visits his primary care physician with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He states that the pain is most severe after physical activity and improves somewhat with rest. He denies any morning stiffness but notes he has difficulty getting up from a seated position and walking long distances. The patient reports no recent trauma, fever, or significant weight loss. His medical history is notable for chronic kidney disease and he has been taking analgesics regularly for pain management. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in both hips, but no visible swelling or redness. A recent pelvic x-ray shows joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis in both hips. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 45-year-old male visits his nephrologist due to recent elevations in his blood pressure and changes in his kidney function tests. He reports a history of mild joint pain in his hands but denies any morning stiffness or swelling. His medical history is significant for obesity and type 2 diabetes, which is managed with metformin. He consumes alcohol occasionally and does not smoke. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 160/95 mmHg, and there is mild bilateral pedal edema. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen. Urinalysis reveals mild proteinuria but no hematuria. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's renal condition?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening around his fingers and intermittent discoloration of his fingers induced by cold exposure, lasting several minutes to an hour. He describes the color changes as turning white to blue and then red upon rewarming. He also reports occasional joint pain and fatigue. On physical examination, there is thickening of the skin on the fingers and hands, and the nailfold capillaries show some changes under magnification. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibody with a nucleolar pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 27-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, fatigue, and joint pain. He reports a recent camping trip in a wooded area where he recalls being bitten by a tick. Since the trip, he has noticed a circular rash on his left thigh that has gradually expanded. On examination, the rash has a central clearing, and he has tenderness and swelling in his knees and wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever, fatigue, and night sweats over the past three months. He has also noticed swelling and tenderness in his neck and groin. He reports intermittent joint pain that worsens with activity and improves with rest. He denies any significant weight loss or night sweats prior to the last few months. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On physical examination, he has palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and inguinal regions. Laboratory studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but normal liver and kidney function tests. A chest X-ray appears unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of acute joint pain. He reports swelling and pain in his knees, ankles, and wrists. He mentions having returned from a trip to the Caribbean three weeks ago. He denies any fever or recent infections. On examination, there is significant swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, with reduced range of motion. Laboratory tests show an elevated ESR and C-reactive protein, but the rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on his face and joint pain. He describes the rash as red and itchy, worsening with sun exposure. Additionally, he has experienced joint stiffness and swelling, particularly in his hands and knees, which improves throughout the day. He reports sores in his mouth but denies any recent fever or weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable aside from seasonal allergies. On examination, there is an erythematous rash across his cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Mild swelling and tenderness are noted in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal anemia, positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test, and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male construction worker presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a noticeable 'slap' when his right foot hits the ground. He reports that the symptoms began insidiously over the past month. He denies any recent trauma to the leg but mentions occasional low back pain. On physical examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot, and the patient is unable to stand on his heels on the affected side. Reflexes are 2+ bilaterally at the knees and 1+ at the right ankle. Sensory examination reveals decreased sensation in the lateral aspect of the right lower leg and dorsum of the foot. The patient also complains of some mild hand and knee pain that is worse in the morning but improves with activity. What is the most likely cause of this patient's foot drop?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with acute onset of joint pain and swelling in his knees, elbows, and wrists. He reports that the symptoms began two days ago after returning from a hiking trip in the Appalachian Mountains. He notes associated fatigue and low-grade fevers but denies any recent illnesses or significant medical history. He has no known allergies and is not on any medications. On physical examination, there is synovitis in the aforementioned joints with mild erythema and tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal a mild leukocytosis and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Joint aspiration for synovial fluid analysis

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in his lower extremities. He reports feeling fatigued and has noticed occasional tingling sensations in his feet. He denies any significant joint pain or swelling, and his medical history is otherwise unremarkable. He works as a software engineer and does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, he has diminished strength in the lower extremities and hyporeflexia at the knees and ankles. Sensory examination reveals diminished vibration sensation in the feet. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe lower back pain that started suddenly after he lifted a heavy box two days ago. He describes the pain as sharp and stabbing, radiating around to the front of his abdomen. He denies any trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history is significant for osteoporosis, for which he has been non-compliant with medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the T12 vertebra, with no neurological deficits noted. His vital signs are stable. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and calcium levels. An X-ray of the thoracic spine reveals a compression fracture at the T12 vertebra. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.C: Immediate CT scan of the spine

question: A 52-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of multiple subcutaneous nodules over his elbows and forearms. He reports that these nodules have been increasing in size and number over the past few months and are sometimes tender. Additionally, he experiences generalized fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. The patient denies any significant joint pain, morning stiffness, or trauma. He has a history of smoking and mild hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, firm, movable nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of the elbows. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) but negative rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. A biopsy of one nodule shows necrotic material surrounded by palisading histiocytes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past 6 months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting for about 2 hours before improving as the day progresses. He also notices swelling in his fingers and wrists. He has no significant past medical history. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. An X-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion and sinus headaches. He reports occasional hemoptysis and has noticed increased fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 5 kg during this period. He also complains of joint pain primarily in his knees and elbows, which he attributes to ongoing arthritis he's had for years. He has a 15-year history of well-controlled hypertension and takes lisinopril. On physical examination, crackles are heard in the lung bases bilaterally, and there is mild tenderness over the maxillary sinuses. The urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. Chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Laboratory findings include an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother because of joint pain for the past 2 weeks. He reports that the pain began in his wrists and has since moved to his knees and ankles. Two weeks ago, he had a sore throat that resolved without treatment. He has had no recent fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, his temperature is 37.5°C (99.5°F), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, and respirations are 16/min. Examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling of the knees and ankles with reduced range of motion. No erythema is noted. Throat culture is negative. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of swelling and pain in multiple joints, including his knees, ankles, and wrists. He returned from a trip to the Caribbean three weeks ago, where he had spent time in rural areas. He reports associated fatigue and low-grade fever but denies any rash or recent infections. On examination, the affected joints are swollen and warm, with limited range of motion due to pain. Routine blood tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Given these findings and his recent travel history, which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past few months. He notes that the pain is primarily in his back and hips and is worse at night. He denies any history of trauma or recent infection. His medical history is notable for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On physical examination, he is found to have mild tenderness over the lumbar spine but no joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL and elevated alkaline phosphatase. A bone scan shows areas of increased radiotracer uptake in the lumbar spine and pelvis. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintended weight loss over the past three months. He also mentions experiencing occasional joint pain, especially in the mornings, but his main concern is feeling constantly tired despite getting adequate sleep. He reports that his appetite has decreased significantly, and he has lost nearly 15 pounds without trying. His medical history is unremarkable, but he notes that his mother had a history of thyroid issues. Laboratory tests reveal normocytic anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 43-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with a two-month history of pain and swelling in his wrists and knees. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks due to joint pain. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness in both wrists and knees. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rheumatoid factor (RF) test is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are elevated. Radiographs of the hands show mild joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue over the past few months. He reports that the pain is mainly in his lower back and hips, and he sometimes feels more tired than usual. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum calcium levels and decreased phosphorus levels. Alkaline phosphatase is also elevated. The patient denies any recent trauma or significant weight loss. He has a history of hypertension controlled with medication. Physical examination shows no joint swelling, and rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances. He describes the pain as a constant ache that affects both sides of his body, including his neck, shoulders, back, and hips. He reports waking up feeling unrefreshed despite sleeping for 8 hours most nights. His history is significant for anxiety and irritable bowel syndrome, but he denies any major medical illnesses. Physical examination reveals tenderness at multiple points over the trapezius, elbows, and knees, without signs of inflammation or swelling. Laboratory tests including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and rheumatoid factor are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 46-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic sinusitis and a persistent cough with occasional hemoptysis over the past few months. He also describes a progressive shortness of breath and fatigue. His medical history includes hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. On physical examination, he has nasal congestion and bilateral crackles on lung auscultation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and a mild anemia. A chest X-ray shows bilateral patchy infiltrates. Urinalysis indicates hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following conditions is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of intense pain in his left big toe over the past year. He describes the pain as sudden in onset and severe, often waking him from sleep. The episodes last for a few days before resolving spontaneously. On physical examination, the left first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, swollen, and tender to touch, with the presence of tophi. He reports no other joint involvement. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Laboratory studies reveal elevated serum uric acid levels. Joint aspiration shows needle-shaped crystals with strong negative birefringence under polarized light. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue, recurrent infections, and joint pain. He notes that over the past few months, he has been feeling increasingly tired and has had two episodes of sinus infections. He works as a software engineer and denies any recent travel. He has no significant past medical history aside from occasional joint stiffness for which he takes over-the-counter analgesics. On physical examination, he is pale and has mild tenderness in his wrists and knees without swelling or erythema. Laboratory workup reveals pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 8.9 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,000/µL, and platelet count of 90,000/µL. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with generalized body aches and fatigue that started a week ago following a viral upper respiratory tract infection. He describes the pain as muscle soreness and joint stiffness, particularly in the mornings. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any regular medications. He denies any skin rashes, fever, or recent travel. On physical examination, there is no joint swelling or erythema, but there is mild tenderness in several muscle groups. Laboratory tests reveal a mild elevation in C-reactive protein and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending forward to pick up a box. He denies any trauma except for the mild strain from lifting. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. His medical history is significant for hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus. He takes lisinopril and metformin. He denies any history of cancer, recent infections, or prolonged steroid use. His diet is low in calcium, and he has not been active due to his sedentary lifestyle. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic vertebrae without any visible deformity. Neurological examination is normal. X-rays of the spine show a wedge-shaped compression of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and unintended weight loss of 10 pounds over the past three months. He reports feeling constantly tired, even after a full night's sleep, and has noticed a decrease in appetite. His medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with lisinopril. He also mentions episodes of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in his hands, which subside after about 30 minutes in the morning. Physical examination reveals pallor. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 9.8 g/dL, low serum iron, low ferritin, and elevated total iron binding capacity (TIBC). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain primarily affecting his knees and wrists, and a rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure. He describes the joint pain as migratory and mentions occasional swelling. The rash is erythematous and has a classic butterfly distribution over his cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. He reports increased fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers but denies any morning stiffness or nodules. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications except for over-the-counter analgesics. Laboratory studies reveal anemia and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old male comes to the clinic complaining of joint pain that seems to migrate from one joint to another. He recalls having a sore throat followed by fever and fatigue about three weeks ago. His mother notes that he has been more tired than usual, and his knees and elbows are now swollen and tender. On examination, there is some warmth and swelling in the joints, and a faint macular rash is observed on his trunk and arms. Vital signs include: heart rate 90/min, blood pressure 110/70 mm Hg, respiratory rate 18/min, and temperature 38.0°C (100.4°F). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past six months. The pain is described as a deep, achy sensation that is most pronounced after prolonged periods of sitting or walking. He denies any history of trauma but mentions a recent increase in physical activity as he started a new exercise regimen. He also reports occasional stiffness in the morning that lasts for about 10 minutes but improves with movement. He denies swelling, redness, or warmth in the joints and has no significant systemic symptoms, such as fever or weight loss. His past medical history is notable for hypercholesterolemia, which is well-controlled with atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the groin area. Range of motion in both hips is slightly limited due to pain, especially on internal rotation. Routine laboratory studies show normal inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the emergency department after he fell on his outstretched left hand while playing basketball. He complains of significant pain and swelling in the wrist and is unable to move it without discomfort. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and decreased range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is performed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent weight gain over the past 6 months. He reports feeling extremely tired and has noticed swelling around his eyes, especially in the mornings, as well as stiffness in his joints that improves throughout the day. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent changes in diet or activity level. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 140/90 mmHg, and there is notable periorbital edema. Laboratory tests reveal low serum sodium and elevated TSH levels. What is the most likely underlying condition causing this patient's symptoms?

A 36-year-old man visits the clinic due to joint pain and fatigue that developed over the past two weeks. He reports a recent hiking trip where he noticed a tick on his leg, which he removed. Since then, he has experienced pain in his knees and elbows and a red rash that started at the site of the tick bite but has since spread. He denies any fever, but mentions feeling more tired than usual. On examination, there is a targetoid rash on his lower leg and mild swelling in both knees. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. He also reports bilateral parotid gland enlargement and mild joint pain. His medical history includes hyperlipidemia, for which he takes atorvastatin. On examination, the patient has xerostomia and conjunctival injection. His temperature is 37.0°C (98.6°F), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, and pulse is 80/min. Laboratory studies show:
Antinuclear antibody: Positive
Rheumatoid factor: Positive
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate: 35 mm/hr
Further evaluation is most likely to reveal which of the following findings?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of worsening bilateral hip pain. He describes the pain as a deep, aching sensation that is exacerbated by weight-bearing activities such as walking and standing for prolonged periods. He denies any significant morning stiffness but mentions occasional discomfort in his knees. His medical history is significant for obesity and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in both hips and tenderness upon palpation of the hip joints. An x-ray of the pelvis shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in both hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash. He notes that he was bitten by a tick approximately three weeks ago while hiking in a wooded area. Since then, he has developed migratory joint pain that initially began in his knees and has now spread to his elbows and wrists. He also reports feeling fatigued and notices a circular, red rash on his thigh where the tick bite occurred. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, the rash is noted to be annular with central clearing, consistent with erythema migrans. Laboratory tests reveal mildly elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 17-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of sore throat, followed by migratory joint pain affecting his knees, wrists, and ankles. The patient describes the joint pain as shifting in location every few days, associated with swelling and redness. He denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes but reports feeling fatigued. He recalls having a 'strep throat' diagnosed by a rapid antigen test at a local urgent care clinic a month ago, for which he received antibiotics. Physical examination reveals mild erythema of the pharynx without exudates and tenderness with swelling of the left knee and right wrist. The patient's temperature is 37.9°C (100.2°F). Laboratory studies show leukocytosis, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and elevated C-reactive protein (CRP). The rapid antigen test for current streptococcal infection is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists that have progressively worsened over the last six months. The patient reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. He has difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning his shirt. His past medical history includes hypertension, for which he is taking lisinopril. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following conditions is most likely associated with the patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and a rash on his cheeks that worsens with sun exposure. He describes the joint pain as intermittent and affecting his wrists and knees bilaterally. He also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. On examination, there is a maculopapular rash across his cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and mild swelling in both wrists. Laboratory tests reveal elevated antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man visits his primary care physician with complaints of progressive weakness in his upper arms and thighs over the past several months. He notes difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He denies any joint pain, but mentions feeling more fatigued than usual. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is well-controlled. On examination, his upper arm and thigh muscles display decreased strength (4/5) bilaterally. A skin rash is noted on his knuckles and there is periungual erythema. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and positive antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of bilateral hand and wrist pain, which he describes as worse in the morning and improving throughout the day. He also reports stiffness lasting over an hour in the mornings. On examination, his metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints are tender and swollen bilaterally. You note several subcutaneous nodules over his elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show juxta-articular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness primarily affecting his fingers and right knee. He reports that the stiffness is worse in the morning and improves as the day progresses. He also has a history of red, scaly patches on his elbows and scalp, for which he uses topical creams. The patient denies any recent infections or new medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the right knee and distal interphalangeal joints, along with silvery plaques on the skin. Laboratory tests show a negative rheumatoid factor and elevated inflammatory markers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with new-onset joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which eases with activity. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 15 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) is strongly positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient?

A 50-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of joint pain in his hands and wrists that has been progressively worsening over the past 6 months. He notes that the pain and stiffness are particularly severe in the morning, often lasting for over an hour before he feels he can move more freely. The patient denies any significant past medical history. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPAs). X-rays of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain that has progressively worsened over the past six months. He notes that the pain is most pronounced in his wrists and fingers and is accompanied by swelling and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His past medical history is unremarkable except for smoking one pack of cigarettes per day for the last 30 years. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 60 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of sudden onset joint pain and swelling affecting both wrists, knees, and ankles. He recently returned from a trip to the Caribbean and recalls experiencing a fever and rash while traveling. He denies any history of joint issues and does not take any medications regularly. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the affected joints, but no visible deformities. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain for the past six months. He reports feeling constantly fatigued and having difficulty sleeping, describing his sleep as unrefreshing. He also experiences occasional headaches and has noted some cognitive difficulties, which he refers to as 'brain fog.' The pain is described as a dull ache that affects his neck, back, and shoulders, and he feels tender when pressed in these areas. His medical history is significant only for mild seasonal allergies. He denies fever, weight loss, or any recent infections. On physical examination, multiple tender points are noted over the trapezius, lateral epicondyle, and gluteal region. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 6-month history of nasal congestion, recurrent sinusitis, and a worsening cough. He describes nasal crusting and occasional small amounts of blood when blowing his nose. Recently, he has noticed fatigue and occasional joint pain in his knees and elbows. His past medical history includes hypertension managed with lisinopril. He denies any tobacco or illicit drug use. On examination, there are bilateral crackles in the lung bases and mild tenderness in the knees without swelling. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and microscopic hematuria. A chest X-ray shows bilateral nodular infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling, particularly in the fingers of his right hand and the toes of his left foot, for the past several months. He also reports scaly, red patches on his elbows and knees that have been present for over a year. He denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or use alcohol. Physical examination reveals asymmetric swelling in the distal interphalangeal joints of the right hand and 'sausage-like' swelling of the second toe on the left foot. Skin examination shows well-demarcated, erythematous plaques with silvery scales. Laboratory tests are notable for a normal rheumatoid factor. X-ray of the hands shows erosions and pencil-in-cup deformities at the distal interphalangeal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his physician with a complaint of progressive right shoulder pain and restricted range of motion over the past several months. He denies any recent trauma or overuse. The pain is described as a dull ache, and he notes difficulty reaching overhead. He also mentions that the pain disrupts his sleep. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia, which are well-controlled with metformin and atorvastatin. He does not smoke but occasionally drinks socially. On physical examination, there is notable stiffness and decreased active and passive range of motion in all directions of the shoulder. No erythema or significant swelling is observed. An x-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of a rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure, joint pain in his knees and elbows, and fatigue. He also reports recurrent oral ulcers over the past few months but denies any significant weight loss or fever. On examination, there is an erythematous, scaly rash across the bridge of his nose and cheeks. Laboratory tests reveal: ANA positive, anti-dsDNA positive, ESR elevated, and decreased complement levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling that started a few hours after playing soccer. He describes the pain as sharp and rates it 8 out of 10. He has no history of trauma to the knee, but mentions that he felt a 'pop' while pivoting on the field. The patient denies fever, chills, or recent infections. He has a past medical history of mild asthma, for which he occasionally uses an inhaler. On examination, the right knee is swollen and tender with a moderate effusion. The range of motion is limited due to pain, and there is a positive Lachman test. An X-ray of the knee is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent dull bone pain, primarily in his pelvis and ribs, for the last few months. He also reports fatigue, frequent urination, and occasional constipation. He has a history of hypertension and is currently on lisinopril. His laboratory results reveal a serum calcium level of 12.2 mg/dL, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and low serum phosphate. X-rays of the pelvis show subperiosteal bone resorption. He denies recent trauma or any new medications. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital ulcers. He reports that the ulcers are persistent and have been occurring over the past year. He also mentions episodes of joint pain affecting both knees and fatigue. He denies any travel history or new sexual partners. Examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity and similar lesions on the genitals. There is no synovitis or joint swelling currently noted. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and elevated acute phase reactants. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past six months. He reports stiffness in the morning lasting about 30 minutes and notes difficulty getting out of a chair due to the pain. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He is an office worker and denies any recent trauma or heavy lifting. He drinks alcohol socially and does not smoke. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in both hips, but no warmth or erythema. Laboratory tests show a normal white blood cell count, normal C-reactive protein, and normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Pelvic x-ray shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in both hips. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to persistent fatigue, bone pain, and recent unintentional weight loss. Over the past few months, he has noticed increased thirst and frequent urination. His medical history is significant for well-controlled hypertension. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals tenderness in the lower back and hips. Laboratory studies show:
Serum calcium 12.5 mg/dL
Serum phosphate 2.2 mg/dL
Serum alkaline phosphatase 156 U/L
Serum parathyroid hormone 85 pg/mL
X-rays of the lumbar spine reveal diffuse osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of progressive fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past six months.
