He also notes some joint pain and occasional morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any significant family history of illness. On examination, the patient appears pale, and there is mild tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, with a mean corpuscular volume of 75 fL, and a ferritin level of 15 ng/mL. His ESR is elevated at 50 mm/hr, and CRP is also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 52-year-old man presents to the orthopedic clinic with a 3-month history of shoulder pain and progressively restricted range of motion in his right shoulder. He works as a carpenter and denies any recent trauma to the area. He notes that the pain worsens with activity and is relieved with rest. There is no morning stiffness, but he experiences discomfort when reaching overhead. He reports no systemic symptoms such as fever, chills, or weight loss. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder and a significant reduction in active and passive range of motion, specifically in abduction and external rotation. X-rays of the shoulder are unremarkable. MRI of the shoulder shows thickening of the coracohumeral ligament and joint capsule. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to joint pain and stiffness, particularly in the mornings, which has been ongoing for the past year. The stiffness lasts for more than an hour and primarily affects his wrists, hands, and knees. His medical history is notable for mild hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. He denies any recent infections or injuries. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory results show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of his hands reveal joint space narrowing and erosions at the affected joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain in his knees and ankles for the past two weeks. He reports feeling fatigued and has experienced a low-grade fever. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. He works as a school teacher and denies any recent travel or infections. On physical examination, there is tenderness in the affected joints but no significant swelling. The skin lesions are erythematous nodules primarily located on the anterior aspects of the lower legs. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with recurrent episodes of fever for the past three months. He reports feeling fatigued and has noticed some weight loss over this period. He has also experienced night sweats but denies any joint pain or swelling. He has no significant past medical history and denies any recent travel or exposure to infectious contacts. On physical examination, he is febrile with no focal findings. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are negative, and a chest X-ray shows no abnormalities. Which of the following is the most likely next step in the evaluation of this patient's condition?

A 28-year-old male presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness that have been progressively worsening over several weeks. He reports swelling in his wrists and fingers, along with a facial rash that becomes more prominent after sun exposure. He denies any recent infections, fevers, or weight loss. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, there is a malar rash across his cheeks, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory studies reveal a positive ANA with a titer of 1:320 and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain, fatigue, and muscle weakness for the past two months. He describes the pain as a dull ache primarily in his lower back and hips, which worsens at night and with activity. He denies any significant trauma but mentions a recent history of polyuria and increased thirst. His medical history includes mild depression, for which he takes sertraline. On physical examination, he appears to be in mild distress due to discomfort, and palpation of the lower back elicits tenderness. Laboratory studies show a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL, a phosphorus level of 2.0 mg/dL, and an elevated parathyroid hormone (PTH) level. His alkaline phosphatase is also elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue, weight loss, and several tender subcutaneous nodules along the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs. He reports having intermittent fevers and night sweats over the past two months. The patient denies joint pain or stiffness. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-managed with medication. Physical examination reveals multiple non-tender subcutaneous nodules and a palpable spleen. Laboratory evaluation shows an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A skin biopsy of one of the nodules demonstrates fibrinoid necrosis and a dense inflammatory infiltrate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of generalized bone pain and fatigue. He has noticed increasing weakness over the last several months and has had difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects. He reports no recent trauma or injuries. Laboratory workup reveals elevated serum calcium levels and decreased phosphate levels. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Physical examination shows mild tenderness over the long bones. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain and progressive difficulty lifting his arm above his head. He denies any history of trauma or overuse, and there is no significant past medical history. The pain is described as a dull ache that worsens with movement and is particularly bothersome at night. On examination, there is limited active and passive range of motion in the left shoulder, especially in abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema. Imaging of the shoulder reveals no acute fractures or dislocations, but there is evidence of joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with a 6-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. He reports difficulty swallowing dry foods and frequently uses eye drops to relieve eye discomfort. He has also noticed a swelling in the area just below his ears, and on examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. He mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue but denies any significant morning stiffness. His medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 43-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and notes that his symptoms improve throughout the day. The pain is symmetric, affecting his wrists, knees, and small joints of both hands. He denies any prior history of joint problems. He works as an accountant and has no significant travel history. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping for the past six months. He reports that the pain is persistent and affects various parts of his body, including his neck, shoulders, and back. The fatigue is relentless, and his sleep is often disrupted by discomfort. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications regularly. On examination, tender points are noted in several areas, but there is no visible joint swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past several months. He has noticed swelling on both sides of his face, which he describes as feeling 'puffy.' The patient denies any recent dental issues or significant changes in his diet. He mentions a history of mild joint pain and occasional fatigue but does not report any morning stiffness or swelling in his joints. On examination, there is bilateral parotid gland enlargement without tenderness. His vital signs are: temperature 36.8°C (98.2°F), blood pressure 118/76 mm Hg, pulse 82/min, and respirations 14/min. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to a new rash on his legs and abdominal pain for the past week. The rash consists of multiple purpuric lesions located primarily on the lower extremities. Additionally, he has experienced intermittent joint pain involving the knees and ankles. His mother reports that he had a sore throat two weeks ago which resolved without treatment. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue for the past few months. He describes the pain as deep and aching, primarily affecting his lower back and ribs. He denies any trauma or recent infections. He also reports increased thirst and occasional constipation. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, well controlled with medication. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. Laboratory tests reveal a serum calcium level of 12.5 mg/dL (normal range: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL) and a parathyroid hormone level of 80 pg/mL (normal range: 10-65 pg/mL). Serum creatinine is 1.1 mg/dL. X-rays of the spine show subperiosteal bone resorption. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending forward to pick up a box. He describes the pain as sharp and localized in the mid-back region. His medical history is significant for hypertension and cigarette smoking, but he denies any recent trauma or falls. On examination, there is marked tenderness over the thoracic vertebrae, but no neurological deficits are observed. X-ray of the spine shows a compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. Laboratory tests reveal normal calcium levels, but a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, which improves as the day progresses. He also notes that he has been feeling more fatigued than usual and has lost 5 pounds unintentionally over the past month. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), along with a positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 63-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, night sweats, and a 5-kg (11-lb) unintentional weight loss. He reports feeling fatigued and has noticed mild joint pain, particularly in his knees and wrists, but denies morning stiffness. On examination, his temperature is 101.5°F (38.6°C), blood pressure is 125/78 mmHg, pulse is 94/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory findings reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but normal white blood cell count. Blood cultures are negative, and a chest x-ray is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient's presentation?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a 3-week history of persistent fever, night sweats, and malaise. He notes having lost 5 pounds unintentionally during this period. His medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. He denies any cough, dyspnea, or recent travel. On examination, he has a temperature of 101.3°F (38.5°C), blood pressure of 130/85 mmHg, pulse of 96/min, and respiratory rate of 18/min. There is mild tenderness in the left knee with no visible swelling or erythema. Initial laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL
White blood cell count 11,000/mm³
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate 70 mm/hr
C-reactive protein 8 mg/dL
Blood cultures have been negative. An autoimmune panel is ordered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 58-year-old male office worker presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness, particularly in his hands and wrists. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning and takes about two hours to subside. He denies any recent injuries or significant physical activity changes. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes managed with metformin. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Blood tests reveal a high erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of intermittent, progressive pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists. He notes that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, lasting approximately 90 minutes, and improves with activity. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, along with the presence of subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man visits the clinic due to diffuse body aches and generalized fatigue that started after recovering from a recent upper respiratory viral infection. He reports that the aches are more pronounced in the mornings but improve as the day progresses. He denies any significant joint swelling or redness but mentions occasional mild stiffness. He works as a software engineer and is currently under considerable stress. On examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. There are no obvious joint deformities, and his vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory studies reveal a mildly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate management step for this patient's current condition?

options.B: Referral to a rheumatologist for further evaluation
options.C: Testing for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.D: Reassurance and symptomatic management with NSAIDs

question: A 60-year-old man presents to his rheumatologist with a 5-year history of chronic bilateral hand and wrist pain, swelling, and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He describes episodes of fatigue and low-grade fever. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Subcutaneous nodules are palpated over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. His medical history is notable for smoking and a family history of autoimmune disease. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and high levels of rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a complaint of unexplained weight gain over the past few months. He reports swelling around his eyes, especially noticeable in the morning, as well as increased stiffness in his joints, predominantly in the hands and knees, which improves as the day progresses. He denies any significant changes in his diet or physical activity levels. On examination, he has notable periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal normal renal function, but his serum albumin is decreased. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of tingling and numbness in his feet and hands. He describes a sensation of pins and needles, particularly in his toes and fingertips, which is worse at night. He also reports occasional hand stiffness and joint pain, primarily affecting the small joints of his hands. He denies any significant weight loss, fever, or rash. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 15 years ago, which is poorly controlled with oral hypoglycemic agents. He also has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove pattern. Joint examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness in the proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory investigations show normal inflammatory markers. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 32-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with chronic lower back pain that has been present for the last 6 months. The pain is described as dull and aching, primarily in the lower back and buttocks, and it improves with activity but worsens with rest, especially in the morning. He reports stiffness lasting more than an hour after waking up. There is no history of trauma or significant weight loss. He denies any radicular pain, bowel or bladder dysfunction, or fever. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility with decreased flexion in the lumbar region. Laboratory findings show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of increasing fatigue, weight gain, and cold intolerance over the last 8 months. He also reports occasional muscle aches and joint pain, particularly in his knees and elbows. He has no significant past medical history. His temperature is 98.0°F (36.7°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 60/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical examination reveals dry skin and modest swelling around the eyes. Laboratory tests show a low serum free T4 level and an elevated TSH level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of recurrent fevers over the past month and generalized fatigue. He reports having noticed some swelling and tenderness in his neck and groin regions. Additionally, he mentions experiencing occasional joint pain in his hands and knees but states that it is not constant. He denies any recent travel or contact with sick individuals. On examination, he is febrile with a temperature of 38.5°C (101.3°F). Palpable, non-tender lymphadenopathy is noted in the cervical and inguinal regions. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A complete blood count is significant for mild anemia and leukocytosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of tingling and numbness in his hands and feet. He reports occasional joint pain, especially in his wrists, and mentions that the tingling sensation worsens at night. He has a history of long-term alcohol use but otherwise is in good health. On physical examination, there is decreased vibratory sensation in both feet and diminished reflexes in the ankles. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to the appearance of a rash and blood in his urine. She reports that he has been experiencing joint pain in his knees and ankles alongside mild abdominal pain for the past week. On physical examination, you observe palpable purpura distributed over the boy's buttocks and posterior aspects of his thighs. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 110/68 mmHg. The patient's medical history is unremarkable, and he has no known allergies. A recent throat infection was reported and resolved without antibiotic treatment. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain in his knees, elbows, and wrists for the past two weeks. He recalls having a sore throat about three weeks ago that resolved without antibiotics. He has been experiencing fatigue and low-grade fevers. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent trauma or infection. On examination, he has a temperature of 100.4°F (38°C) and mild erythema and swelling over the affected joints. His heart sounds reveal a new systolic murmur best heard at the apex. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with concerns about recent increases in his blood pressure. He reports that over the last six months, he has experienced episodic headaches and fatigue. He also mentions recent swelling in his ankles and occasional joint pain in his wrists and knees. His medical history includes hyperlipidemia, but he has no known history of kidney disease. His blood pressure is recorded at 168/98 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine level and mild proteinuria. Further laboratory studies show elevated levels of urea nitrogen. A renal ultrasound is performed, showing bilateral small kidneys. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 45-year-old male presents to his primary care provider with complaints of joint pain and swelling in his wrists and both hands over the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves as the day progresses. He denies any recent infections or trauma. His medical history includes seasonal allergies, and he is otherwise healthy. His family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in his mother. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of painful lumps under his skin, primarily located over his elbows and knees. He also reports a fever and feeling more fatigued than usual over the past few weeks. He has a history of hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. Additionally, he has noted some mild joint pain but attributes this to aging. Physical examination reveals several firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, including an increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A biopsy of one of the nodules shows granulomatous inflammation with central necrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe lower back pain after bending over to pick up a heavy object. The pain is sharp and localized to the midline of his lumbar region. He denies any radiation to the legs, numbness, or weakness. He has a history of prostate cancer in remission and is currently taking calcium and vitamin D supplements. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation over the L2 vertebra with no paraspinal muscle tenderness. Neurological examination is unremarkable. An X-ray of the lumbar spine reveals a compression fracture at L2. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and joint stiffness over the past three months. He notes that the stiffness occurs mostly in the morning and usually improves by midday. He denies any fever, recent changes in diet, or changes in medication. His past medical history includes well-controlled hypothyroidism for which he takes levothyroxine. Physical examination reveals mild pitting edema in the lower extremities and puffiness around the eyes. He has mild tenderness in the small joints of the hands and wrists, but no significant swelling or deformity. Laboratory tests reveal normal thyroid function tests, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and normal renal function. What is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with severe pain, swelling, and redness in his right big toe that started last night. This is his third such episode in the past year. The patient has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. On examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is erythematous, warm, and exquisitely tender to touch. A subcutaneous nodule is palpable over the olecranon. Serum uric acid is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with persistent fatigue and swelling in his legs for the past 3 months. He reports a recent increase in blood pressure readings, despite previously being normotensive. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint pain in his hands, which he attributes to old sports injuries. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated serum creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL and a blood urea nitrogen of 45 mg/dL. Urinalysis demonstrates proteinuria and hematuria without casts. His temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 165/95 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. A renal ultrasound is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's renal abnormalities?

A 35-year-old male comes to the clinic due to fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the last two months. He describes pain and swelling in the small joints of his hands and wrists, which worsens in the morning and improves throughout the day. He also reports feeling more tired than usual, even after adequate sleep, and has noticed several unexplained bruises on his legs. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, he appears pale, and there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 2,300/mm3, and platelet count of 70,000/mm3. Further tests show elevated lactate dehydrogenase and low haptoglobin levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a new onset of a lacy, purplish rash on his legs and recurrent episodes of leg pain. He mentions experiencing similar episodes over the past year, often accompanied by swelling and redness in the affected area. He also reports having had several unexplained episodes of shortness of breath and chest pain, for which he did not seek medical attention. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, which he manages with lisinopril. Family history reveals several relatives with autoimmune disorders. On examination, his temperature is 98.7°F (37.1°C), blood pressure is 138/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 18/min. A basic metabolic panel is unremarkable, but laboratory studies show elevated D-dimer levels. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, persistent fatigue, and difficulty sleeping for the past six months. He describes the pain as dull and aching, affecting both sides of his body, including his neck, shoulders, and hips. He has a history of depression and irritable bowel syndrome, for which he takes sertraline and dicyclomine. His vital signs are within normal limits, and his physical examination reveals multiple tender points upon palpation without any signs of joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests including CBC, ESR, and CRP are unremarkable. Rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies are negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has progressively worsened over the past six months. He describes the pain as a deep ache that is present throughout the day and is exacerbated by prolonged walking or standing. The patient denies any morning stiffness, but notes difficulty in climbing stairs and getting out of chairs. He has a history of obesity and hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. His vital signs are within normal limits. On physical examination, there is limited internal rotation of both hips and tenderness over the greater trochanteric region bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the hips reveals subchondral sclerosis and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that intensifies with activity and improves with rest. He denies any specific trauma or injury to the area. Additionally, he reports feeling more tired than usual but has no significant morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion in both hips, but no swelling or erythema is observed. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers. An x-ray of the pelvis shows narrowing of the joint space in both hips. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint swelling and morning stiffness lasting about two hours. He reports swelling and pain in his wrists, metacarpophalangeal joints, and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. He denies any fever or recent infections. His medical history is significant for hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and synovitis noted in the affected joints. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are both positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, fatigue, and generalized malaise. He reports having night sweats and a 5-pound weight loss over this period. He also describes vague joint discomfort, particularly in the morning, but no specific joint swelling or acute pain. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. He has never smoked and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, his temperature is 38.3°C (100.9°F), blood pressure is 130/85 mm Hg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. There are no significant findings on joint examination. Initial laboratory studies reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate, with normal white blood cell count and liver function tests. Blood cultures are negative. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.D: Order a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
options.E: Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation
options.G: Conduct a purified protein derivative (PPD) test

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, along with swelling and pain in both wrists and several proximal interphalangeal joints of the hands. He reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. He denies any known trauma or recent infections. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the wrists and fingers. Laboratory studies reveal an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr and a positive rheumatoid factor. Anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are also positive. Radiographs of the hands show soft tissue swelling and periarticular osteopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with a complaint of difficulty walking and a sensation of weakness in his right foot for the past 3 weeks. He reports tripping over his right foot and has noticed that he cannot lift the front part of his foot when walking. He denies any recent trauma or history of diabetes. The patient also mentions occasional fatigue and mild joint stiffness in the mornings, which improves throughout the day. On physical examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion in the right foot, and a positive Trendelenburg sign is noted. A nerve conduction study is ordered. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain that is worse in the morning and improves with activity. He also reports stiffness in his back that lasts for over an hour after waking up. He denies any significant trauma, fever, or significant weight loss. He mentions occasional fatigue and difficulty bending forward. Past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals limited range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic due to recurrent episodes of joint pain in his right big toe over the past year. He describes the pain as excruciating and notes that it often flares after he drinks beer or consumes seafood. The patient has a history of hypertension managed with hydrochlorothiazide. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. On physical examination, the right first metatarsophalangeal joint is swollen, red, and tender to touch. There are visible nodules on his ear helix and elbow. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum uric acid levels. An X-ray of the foot shows erosions with overhanging edges at the affected joint. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man arrives at the emergency department with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling that began after playing basketball. He reports no prior history of knee problems but mentions a similar episode affecting his left ankle several months ago, which resolved spontaneously after a few days. On examination, the right knee is erythematous, warm, and tender with a moderate effusion. The range of motion is limited due to pain. His temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 128/82 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. A rapid strep test is negative. Laboratory results show a mildly elevated white blood cell count and elevated C-reactive protein levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Order an MRI of the knee
options.E: Test for high levels of rheumatoid factor
options.G: Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation

question: A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of persistent fatigue over the past 6 months. He reports feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and describes intermittent muscle weakness. The patient also notes mild weight gain and occasional joint pain, especially in his hands. He denies any recent cold intolerance, but his wife mentions he seems less energetic than before. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. On examination, his skin appears dry. Laboratory tests reveal elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (T4) levels. Inflammatory markers, including ESR and CRP, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain that has been migrating from his knees to his wrists and elbows over the past week. He reports a sore throat about two weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. On examination, there is mild swelling and redness around the affected joints. He does not recall any recent injuries and denies any family history of similar symptoms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain after bending forward to pick up a heavy object. He describes the pain as sharp and incapacitating. His medical history is significant for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and occasional use of corticosteroids for exacerbations. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the thoracic spine, and he is unable to sit or stand without support. An X-ray of the spine reveals a compression fracture of the T10 vertebra. Laboratory tests show normal calcium and phosphate levels. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition contributing to the patient's presentation?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic sinusitis, cough, and hemoptysis. He reports occasional blood in his urine and has felt increasingly fatigued. His medical history includes seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the maxillary sinuses, mild inspiratory crackles in the lower lung fields, and 2+ pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen, along with an increased erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Urinalysis reveals red blood cell casts and mild proteinuria. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Which of the following laboratory tests is most likely to confirm the underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of progressive muscle weakness over the past several months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He denies joint pain or skin rashes. His medical history is unremarkable except for well-controlled hypertension. Physical examination reveals symmetric weakness of the proximal muscles in the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase levels. Electromyography shows myopathic changes. A muscle biopsy is performed and reveals perifascicular atrophy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a recent diagnosis of hypertension. He mentions episodes of fatigue and occasional ankle swelling over the past month. He denies any significant medical history or medication use. Physical examination reveals blood pressure of 155/95 mmHg, mild periorbital edema, and bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatinine and blood urea nitrogen, with blood tests revealing mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. A urinalysis shows proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.B: Screen for high levels of rheumatoid factor

question: A 28-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash on his face and joint pain. The facial rash appeared a few weeks ago, worsens with sun exposure, and is described as a butterfly-shaped rash over the cheeks and bridge of the nose sparing the nasolabial folds. He also reports joint pain and swelling in his wrists and knees. He denies any recent fever or weight loss. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is a notable erythematous rash in a malar distribution and tenderness of the wrists and knees with mild swelling. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man visits the clinic due to chronic neck pain and tingling sensations in his right arm for the past 4 months. He reports that the symptoms often intensify after extended periods of typing at his desk job, leading to occasional weakness in grip strength. His medical history includes mild hypertension, managed with diet and exercise, and no history of trauma. On examination, he has limited range of motion in cervical rotation. Muscle strength is 4/5 in the right hand, and there is a reduced pinprick sensation along the medial aspect of the right forearm. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ in the biceps and triceps. A cervical spine MRI shows disc osteophyte complex at the C6-C7 level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with persistent swelling of the neck, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss over the last three months. He also reports fatigue and occasional fever. His family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. On examination, he has multiple enlarged, non-tender cervical lymph nodes and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and lactate dehydrogenase (LDH). A biopsy of one of the enlarged lymph nodes shows Reed-Sternberg cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of right shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a deep, aching sensation that worsens with movement and is accompanied by stiffness, especially in the mornings. The patient denies any history of trauma or overuse. Physical examination reveals restricted active and passive range of motion in all directions at the shoulder joint, with tenderness to palpation over the anterior and lateral aspects. There is no erythema, warmth, or swelling noted. The patient has a history of controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. An X-ray of the shoulder shows no fractures or dislocations. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male visits his dermatologist due to progressive skin tightening and changes in his fingertips. He mentions experiencing episodes of finger discoloration, especially when exposed to cold, which resolves after warming. Over the past few months, he has also felt tired and notices some joint stiffness in the mornings lasting about 30 minutes. He denies joint swelling or significant pain. Physical examination reveals thickened, shiny skin over the fingers and hands, and several telangiectasias are noted on his face. Nailfold capillaroscopy shows dilated and irregular capillaries. Laboratory tests demonstrate positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) but are negative for rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. He also reports persistent swelling in his neck and underarms. On examination, multiple enlarged, non-tender lymph nodes are palpable in the cervical and axillary regions. He denies any recent infections or travel history. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. Laboratory findings reveal elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels and normocytic anemia. A chest X-ray shows mediastinal lymphadenopathy. A biopsy of the cervical lymph node is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man visits the clinic due to experiencing diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He recalls having a fever and sore throat about three weeks ago, which resolved without intervention. He reports generalized muscle soreness and joint pain, particularly in the wrists and knees, which is more pronounced in the morning and improves slightly with activity. He denies any swelling or redness in the joints. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. Physical examination reveals tenderness over several muscle groups but no joint swelling or deformities. Laboratory studies show a mildly elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) level but normal rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies.

What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic complaining of joint pain and stiffness over the past 3 months. He notes that the pain is most severe in the mornings, making it difficult to get out of bed, but improves as the day progresses. On examination, there is symmetric swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Radiographs show periarticular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking and lower extremity weakness over the past six months. He notes occasional joint pain and fatigue but denies numbness or tingling. His past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. Neurological examination reveals decreased strength in the lower limbs, particularly in the proximal muscles, but normal deep tendon reflexes. Electromyography (EMG) shows myopathic changes. Laboratory tests indicate elevated creatine kinase (CK) levels and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a two-day history of a rash on his legs and blood in his urine. He reports recent joint pain, particularly in his knees and ankles, and fatigue. On further questioning, he mentions having had an upper respiratory infection a week ago. On physical examination, he has palpable purpura on his lower extremities and mild swelling of his knees and ankles. Laboratory studies reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria on urinalysis. Blood tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but his renal function tests are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Vasculitis related to systemic lupus erythematosus

question: A 72-year-old man presents to the clinic with a sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the lower back, making it difficult for him to stand or walk. His medical history includes chronic hypertension and osteopenia. On examination, there is tenderness over the lumbar spine and a loss of height since his last visit. Neurological examination of the lower extremities is normal. An x-ray of the lumbar spine is performed, revealing a loss of vertebral height in L3. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers and no evidence of infection. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 5-year history of chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. The pain is associated with morning stiffness lasting over an hour and improves with activity. He has a history of smoking and hypertension, both of which are well-controlled. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Subcutaneous nodules are palpable over the extensor surfaces of the forearms. Laboratory tests reveal positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 28-year-old male visits the dermatology clinic due to a rash that develops after exposure to sunlight. He reports that the rash is on his face and arms and is accompanied by joint pain, particularly in his knees and wrists. He also mentions experiencing mouth sores that make eating uncomfortable. The symptoms have persisted for the past 6 weeks, and he feels increasingly fatigued. He denies any recent infections or new medications. His past medical history is unremarkable. Family history is significant for autoimmune diseases, including lupus in his mother. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash distributed over the cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of skin tightening over his fingers and hands that he has noticed over the past several months. He reports that his fingers turn white and become painful when exposed to cold. He also mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue, but denies any fevers or weight loss. His medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease. On examination, there is thickening of the skin over the fingers and hands, and digital ulcerations are observed. His laboratory tests show positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 57-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a dragging sensation in his right foot for the past month. He also notes some mild back pain that occasionally radiates down his right leg. On physical exam, there is weakness in ankle dorsiflexion and a high-stepping gait is observed. Reflexes are diminished in the right ankle. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history is significant for long-standing hypertension and he takes lisinopril. Imaging of the lumbar spine shows mild degenerative changes with disc bulging. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent episodes of painful, bluish skin discoloration on his legs for the past year. He also reports episodes of swelling and discomfort in his legs and has experienced several unexplained blood clots despite no significant personal or family history. He denies any recent trauma or prolonged immobilization. On examination, the patient exhibits livedo reticularis on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal mild thrombocytopenia and elevated levels of inflammatory markers. Which of the following laboratory findings is most likely to be associated with this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care provider with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. He reports that the pain started gradually and has worsened over time, limiting his ability to lift his arm above his head. He denies any history of trauma, fever, or weight loss, but notes some mild fatigue. He works as a bartender and finds it increasingly difficult to perform tasks like reaching for high shelves. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the right shoulder with a reduced range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. His temperature is 98.2°F (36.8°C), blood pressure is 122/78 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 16/min. An x-ray of the shoulder shows narrowing of the subacromial space. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 37-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and occasional fevers over the past few months. He describes the joint pain as primarily affecting his wrists and knees, and it is more pronounced in the morning, lasting about 30 minutes before improving during the day. He denies any significant past medical history and has not been taking any medications. On examination, he appears pale, and there is tenderness and mild swelling noted in the wrists and knees bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal: Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL, White blood cell count 3,200/mm3, Platelet count 110,000/mm3, Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) 50 mm/h, and a negative rheumatoid factor. Peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia and neutropenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past several months. He also reports recent episodes of fever and night sweats. Upon physical examination, mild hepatosplenomegaly is noted. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia, with low hemoglobin, leukocytes, and platelets. The peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia and occasional immature white blood cells. The patient denies any significant past medical history and has not been on any medications. Further inquiries indicate no recent travel or exposure to known infections. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 75-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute back pain after lifting a heavy box. He describes the pain as severe and localized to his lower back. He denies any trauma or recent falls. His medical history is significant for long-term corticosteroid use for inflammatory arthritis. On examination, there is tenderness over the lumbar vertebrae, but no neurological deficits. An X-ray of the spine reveals a compression fracture of the L2 vertebra. His laboratory results show normal calcium and phosphate levels, but a slight elevation in inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition contributing to this patient's vertebral fracture?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of bone pain and fatigue for the past three months. He reports that the pain is most noticeable in his lower back and ribs. He has also experienced increased thirst and frequent urination. He denies any recent trauma or new medications. His medical history is only significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness over the ribs and a normal range of motion in all joints. Laboratory tests show elevated serum calcium levels, decreased phosphate, and elevated alkaline phosphatase. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He also reports mild joint pain in his hands, which he attributes to his work as a carpenter. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. His medical history is notable for seasonal allergies. On examination, he has symmetric weakness of the proximal muscles in his shoulders and hips. There is no muscle tenderness or swelling, and his joint exam is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show elevated creatine kinase (CK) levels and positive anti-nuclear antibodies (ANA). An electromyography (EMG) is performed and shows muscle membrane irritability. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressive bilateral hip pain over the past 8 months. He describes the pain as deep and aching, which worsens with weight-bearing activities and improves with rest. The patient denies any morning stiffness or systemic symptoms. He has a history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia, managed with lisinopril and atorvastatin, respectively. He is a retired construction worker. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the pelvis reveals joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis in the hip joints. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.E: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a complaint of joint pain and a rash on his face. He reports that the rash worsens with sun exposure and describes it as a red, butterfly-shaped rash over his cheeks and nose. The joint pain has been persistent for the past few weeks, affecting his wrists and knees. He denies any significant morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. Physical examination reveals a well-demarcated malar rash and tenderness in his wrists without swelling. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and leukopenia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male comes to the clinic complaining of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and joint stiffness that has been progressively worsening over the last three months. He reports that his symptoms are more pronounced in the morning and improve somewhat throughout the day. The patient denies any fever, rash, or significant pain, but notes some fatigue. He has a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. On physical examination, there is puffiness around the eyes and mild bilateral pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal low serum albumin and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's presentation?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, weight gain, and joint pain over the past six months. He describes the joint pain as diffuse and affecting both small and large joints, but denies any morning stiffness. He also reports feeling cold more often and notes some thinning of his hair. His medical history is notable for hypertension, treated with lisinopril. On examination, his heart rate is 56/min, blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, and he appears tired with dry skin. Laboratory tests reveal elevated TSH and low free T4 levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of skin tightening primarily affecting his fingers. He reports that his fingers turn white and become painful in cold weather, and he experiences episodes of finger swelling. He also notes some joint pain and stiffness in the mornings that improve as the day progresses. His medical history is significant for gastroesophageal reflux disease. Physical examination reveals thickened, taut skin over the fingers and hands, and several telangiectasias on the face. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe headaches, fatigue, and swelling around his ankles over the past several weeks. He also mentions some joint pain and stiffness in his fingers during the morning that resolves after an hour. His medical history includes well-controlled diabetes. On examination, his blood pressure is 175/100 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal a serum creatinine of 3.2 mg/dL and blood urea nitrogen of 85 mg/dL. Urinalysis shows 2+ proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. An abdominal ultrasound indicates bilateral small kidneys. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's current condition?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of pain and stiffness in his fingers and wrists for the past six months. He reports that the stiffness is most pronounced in the morning, often lasting more than an hour before improving with activity. He also notes occasional swelling of his joints and fatigue. He denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of skin lesions and joint pain. He reports that the lesions first appeared on his elbows and knees as scaly, erythematous plaques, which have gradually spread. Additionally, he has been experiencing pain and swelling in his right knee and left wrist, with morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. He denies fevers or weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. Family history reveals that his father has a similar skin condition. On physical exam, well-demarcated plaques with silvery scales are noted on the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees. The right knee is swollen and tender. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal rheumatoid factor. Joint radiographs reveal soft tissue swelling and erosions at the distal interphalangeal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping for the past six months. He reports waking up feeling unrefreshed despite getting 8 hours of sleep each night. His muscle pain is described as a deep, aching sensation that affects both sides of his body and seems to have worsened over time. He denies any swelling in his joints but feels stiff every morning for about 30 minutes. He has tried over-the-counter pain relievers with minimal relief. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any chronic medications. On examination, multiple tender points are noted in all four quadrants of his body without any signs of swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal normal ESR, CRP, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of lower back pain that has progressively worsened. He notes morning stiffness that improves with activity but worsens with prolonged inactivity. He has no significant past medical history, and family history is negative for autoimmune disorders. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility, particularly with lumbar flexion, and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies show elevated ESR and CRP, but rheumatoid factor is negative. HLA-B27 testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute pain and swelling in his right knee that began after a weekend hiking trip. He describes the pain as severe and states it started suddenly while descending a steep hill. He denies any history of knee injury, fever, or recent illness. On examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation with a limited range of motion. An X-ray of the knee is unremarkable, but joint aspiration reveals cloudy synovial fluid with a high white blood cell count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and stiffness in his hands and wrists for the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He denies any recent trauma or infections. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He works as an accountant and is a non-smoker. On physical examination, there is synovitis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with reduced grip strength. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Serology tests show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (anti-CCP). X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and stiffness in his hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past 6 months. He notes the stiffness is worst in the morning, lasting over an hour before improving as the day progresses. He works as an accountant and reports difficulty typing due to pain. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with limited range of motion. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past few months. He reports feeling unusually tired and having frequent nosebleeds, alongside swelling and pain in his wrists and knees. His medical history is significant for hypertension and a recent upper respiratory tract infection. He denies any significant alcohol use or recent travel. A physical examination reveals pallor and petechiae on the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show pancytopenia. A bone marrow biopsy demonstrates hypocellularity with fatty infiltration. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling. He reports morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes and describes the pain as asymmetrically affecting his fingers and toes. On examination, there are tender, swollen joints in the distal interphalangeal joints of his right hand and pitting of the nails. There are also scaly, erythematous plaques on his elbows and knees. His medical history is notable for hypertension, well controlled with medication. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers but negative rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic pain and swelling in both hands and wrists that have worsened over the past year. He reports stiffness in the morning lasting over an hour and the presence of firm nodules under the skin near his elbows. He has a history of smoking and denies any recent trauma or overuse of his hands. On physical examination, the patient has swollen and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-citrullinated protein antibodies. Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic due to weight gain and swelling around his eyes that have been progressively worsening over the past two months. He also reports stiffness in his joints, particularly in the morning, which subsides as the day progresses. He denies any recent changes in his diet or exercise routine. He has a history of seasonal allergies but is otherwise healthy. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical examination reveals periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show elevated levels of urea nitrogen and creatinine, along with hypoalbuminemia. Urinalysis reveals 4+ proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man with a history of recurrent deep vein thromboses presents to the clinic with a persistent reticular, net-like rash on his legs. He reports episodes of joint pain and fatigue over the past few months. He is a non-smoker and denies recent travel or immobilization. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers and normal kidney function. Antinuclear antibody (ANA) test is negative, but further testing shows the presence of anticardiolipin antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while playing tennis. He describes immediate pain and swelling, but denies any numbness or tingling. He has a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes and hypertension. On examination, the right wrist shows swelling and tenderness, especially over the distal radius. There is limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination of the hand is unremarkable. An x-ray of the wrist is ordered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management for this patient?

options.C: Order a CT scan of the wrist
options.F: Obtain blood tests for rheumatoid factor

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs, which have developed over the past few months. He reports feeling fatigued and has experienced some weight loss, but attributes this to stress at work. He also mentions occasional joint pain in his wrists and knees, which he dismisses as minor aches. On examination, the nodules are firm and non-tender. Further evaluation reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents to the emergency department with wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while playing basketball. He reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist area and is unable to move his wrist due to the severe pain. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint stiffness in the mornings, which resolves within an hour. Physical examination reveals tenderness and swelling over the distal radius. An x-ray of the wrist is shown in Figure A. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive weakness in his shoulders and hips over the past few months. He mentions difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. He denies any pain or numbness. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional joint pain, which he attributes to previous sports injuries. He works as a construction manager and has not had any recent changes in his work routine. On physical examination, there is noted weakness in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal elevated levels of creatine kinase and a normal rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth that have developed over the past six months. He also reports fatigue and occasional joint pain in his hands and knees. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, controlled with medication. On physical examination, his parotid glands are enlarged bilaterally without tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of numbness and tingling in his hands and feet that have gradually worsened over the past six months. He describes a "glove and stocking" pattern of sensory disturbance. He has a history of poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus for 15 years. He reports occasional joint pain, mostly in his hands and wrists, but attributes it to his manual labor job. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a distal symmetrical distribution in the upper and lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished at the ankle. Laboratory tests reveal an HbA1c of 9.2%. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of right shoulder pain and restricted range of motion for the past three months. He reports difficulty reaching overhead or tucking in his shirt behind his back. There is no history of trauma or recent illness. His past medical history includes well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and mild hypertension. He denies any joint swelling or systemic symptoms such as fever or weight loss. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior aspect of the shoulder, limited active and passive range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation, and no visible swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal a normal white blood cell count, elevated C-reactive protein, and negative rheumatoid factor. An x-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. What is the next best step in management for this patient’s most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and recent development of genital ulcers. He reports that the oral ulcers have been occurring for the past few years, initially resolving on their own, but have become more frequent and painful. He also notes occasional joint pain, primarily affecting his knees and ankles, which sometimes swell and become warm. He denies any recent travel or new sexual partners. His medical history is significant for episodic eye pain and redness. On examination, the patient is in no acute distress. Oral examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers on the buccal mucosa. Genital examination shows shallow, well-demarcated ulcers on the scrotum. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe mid-back pain after lifting a heavy box at home. The pain is sharp, localized, and exacerbated by movement. His medical history is notable for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease and long-term corticosteroid use. He denies trauma or recent falls. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the T8 vertebra with no neurological deficits. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count and metabolic panel, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is noted. An x-ray of the thoracic spine reveals a wedge-shaped compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.G: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the spine

question: A 35-year-old man presents to the emergency department with complaints of joint pain, a rash, and mouth ulcers that have developed over the past month. He reports the rash worsens with sun exposure, and he has experienced fatigue and low-grade fevers during this period. He denies any recent illness, significant medical history, or current medications. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash over his cheeks and bridge of the nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and several shallow ulcers in the oral mucosa. His joints show mild swelling and tenderness. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test with elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the emergency department with complaints of a purplish, net-like rash on his legs and recurrent episodes of pain and swelling in his lower extremities. He reports having experienced episodes of deep vein thrombosis in the past year, for which he has been on intermittent anticoagulation therapy. His medical history is otherwise unremarkable, except for occasional joint pain in his fingers and knees, which he attributes to old sports injuries. He denies any significant family history of clotting disorders or autoimmune diseases. On examination, he appears well but has livedo reticularis on both lower limbs. Laboratory tests show a prolonged activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) and positive lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of diffuse body aches and joint pain. He reports that these symptoms started about a week after recovering from a viral illness characterized by fever, sore throat, and fatigue. The joint pain is primarily in his knees and elbows and is accompanied by occasional swelling and stiffness. He denies any previous history of joint issues and has no known allergies. His temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C), blood pressure is 120/75 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, respirations are 18/min, and oxygen saturation is 99% on room air. Physical exam reveals tenderness and mild swelling in the affected joints but no erythema. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 36-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of multiple subcutaneous nodules over his elbows and forearms that have developed over the last 3 months. He reports feeling increasingly fatigued and experiencing low-grade fevers during this period. Additionally, he notes intermittent episodes of joint pain, primarily affecting his wrists and knees. He denies any history of trauma to the affected areas. On examination, there are firm, non-tender nodules over the extensor surfaces of his elbows. Laboratory workup reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain that has been worsening over the past two weeks. He describes the nodules as tender and notes that they initially appeared as small, firm bumps that grew over time. He denies any recent trauma, insect bites, or new medications. He reports some fatigue and low-grade fever. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not taking any medications. On physical examination, there are several erythematous nodules on his anterior lower legs. His knee joints are mildly swollen and tender without obvious effusion.
