Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest X-ray reveals bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to his physician with unexplained weight gain over the past six months, accompanied by periorbital edema and stiffness in multiple joints, particularly in the mornings. He reports no significant changes in diet or physical activity. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On examination, there is puffiness around the eyes and mild synovitis in the wrists and knees. Laboratory tests reveal normal renal function and elevated inflammatory markers. Thyroid function tests show low T4 and elevated TSH. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man visits his physician because of unexplained weight gain and swelling around his eyes for the past two months. He also reports joint stiffness in the morning lasting about an hour and general fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent changes in diet or exercise routine. On examination, he has puffy periorbital edema and mild swelling in his hands. Laboratory studies reveal a serum creatinine of 1.4 mg/dL, serum albumin of 2.8 g/dL, and elevated inflammatory markers. Urinalysis shows 4+ proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and a 5 kg weight loss over the past three months. He also reports occasional joint pain, mainly in the hands and knees, but denies any morning stiffness. His past medical history is notable for well-controlled hypertension. He denies any recent travel or changes in diet, and he does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctiva and mild hepatomegaly. Laboratory results show hemoglobin at 9.5 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume 78 fL, and elevated ferritin levels. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is 65 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 56-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of new-onset symmetrical swelling and pain in both his hands and wrists over the past three months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour each day. His past medical history includes controlled hypertension, and he denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). His rheumatoid factor (RF) is positive, and anti-citrullinated protein antibodies (ACPA) are elevated. A plain radiograph of the hands shows soft tissue swelling and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 50-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves with activity. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His past medical history is unremarkable except for mild hypertension. On examination, there is symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are both positive. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of fever, night sweats, and fatigue over the past two months. He reports occasional joint discomfort and has noticed some swelling in his neck. He denies any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. On examination, he has palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. His temperature is 100.8°F (38.2°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 16/min. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and mild anemia. A biopsy of one of the lymph nodes is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent sinus congestion and occasional nosebleeds. He reports chronic fatigue and a recent onset of cough with blood-tinged sputum. He also mentions experiencing mild joint pain in his knees and elbows. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. Physical examination shows tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and crackles in the lung bases. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and a urinalysis shows red blood cell casts. Serologic tests for anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibodies (ANCA) are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in his feet, which has now started affecting his hands. He describes a 'pins and needles' sensation but denies any weakness or changes in muscle bulk. His medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes and hypertension, both well-managed with medication. He denies alcohol use and reports no recent infections. On examination, there is decreased sensation to light touch in a stocking-glove distribution, but motor strength is preserved. Deep tendon reflexes are reduced in the ankles but normal at the knees. Laboratory tests reveal normal renal function and no evidence of vitamin deficiencies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old boy presents to the clinic with joint pain that has been moving from joint to joint over the past week. He initially had pain in his knees, which then shifted to his ankles, and now involves his wrists. He reports a sore throat approximately three weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. He denies any history of trauma. His temperature is 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg, pulse is 90/min, and respirations are 18/min. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the wrists and ankles. Cardiac auscultation reveals a new, soft systolic murmur best heard at the apex. Laboratory studies show an elevated C-reactive protein and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling off a ladder while painting his house. He reports landing directly on his outstretched hand. The pain is severe and he is unable to use the wrist. He has a history of mild hypertension managed with diet and exercise. He denies any similar injuries or joint issues in the past. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness over the distal radius with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist shows a fracture of the distal radius with mild displacement. Which of the following is the next best step in management?

A 32-year-old male who is an avid runner presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling that started immediately after completing a half-marathon. He describes the pain as sharp and states that it worsens with movement. The patient denies any direct trauma to the knee. His past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional tension headaches. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to palpation, with limited range of motion due to pain. An x-ray of the knee shows soft tissue swelling but no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory tests reveal a normal white blood cell count and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with concerns about persistent skin changes and joint pain. He reports a three-month history of red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees. He also notes stiffness and swelling in his right index finger and left ankle that seems to worsen after periods of inactivity but improves somewhat with movement. He denies any significant morning stiffness. His family history is positive for psoriasis in his brother. On physical exam, erythematous plaques with silvery scales are noted on the extensor surfaces, and the involved joints show swelling and tenderness. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, unintentional weight loss of 15 pounds over the last three months, and generalized weakness. He also reports occasional joint discomfort but denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 15 minutes. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, he appears pale. Laboratory investigations reveal hemoglobin of 10 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 70 fL, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Further testing shows a positive fecal occult blood test. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.A: Iron deficiency anemia due to dietary insufficiency

question:
A 54-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of numbness and tingling in his feet that has been worsening over the past 6 months. He also reports a recent difficulty in maintaining balance when walking. He denies any alcohol use. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 5 years ago, which he admits has been poorly controlled. He also notes occasional joint pain in his hands, which he attributes to "old age." He is taking metformin for his diabetes. On physical examination, there is decreased sensation to pinprick and vibration in a stocking-glove distribution. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished at the ankles and knees. Laboratory studies show:
Hemoglobin A1c 9.2%
Fasting glucose 160 mg/dL
Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 38-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of intermittent fever, night sweats, and generalized malaise. He reports a significant weight loss of approximately 5 kg over the same period. He denies any recent travel or exposure to sick contacts. His medical history is notable for occasional joint pain in his hands, but he has never been formally diagnosed with any chronic conditions. On examination, he is febrile with a temperature of 38.3°C (100.9°F). Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures are pending. A thorough clinical examination and initial imaging studies have not identified a clear source of infection. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the evaluation of this patient's fever of unknown origin?

A 39-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of joint pain and swelling in his hands and wrists. He reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 90 minutes and has difficulty performing his job as a computer programmer due to the pain. His past medical history is unremarkable and he takes ibuprofen as needed for pain relief. He denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical examination, there is tenderness and swelling of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 45 mm/hr, C-reactive protein (CRP) of 12 mg/L, and positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of intermittent fevers, fatigue, and weight loss over the past three months. He also reports the presence of multiple, painless, firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms and legs. He has a history of occasional joint pain, but no significant swelling. He denies any morning stiffness. On examination, you note the subcutaneous nodules and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers. An excisional biopsy of one of the nodules is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of a purplish, net-like rash on his legs and recurrent episodes of leg pain and swelling over the past six months. He reports that the rash becomes more prominent in colder temperatures. His medical history is significant for two previous deep vein thromboses, one of which required hospitalization. He denies any joint pain or stiffness. Laboratory studies reveal elevated D-dimer levels and a prolonged partial thromboplastin time (PTT). A biopsy of the skin lesion shows intravascular thrombi without vasculitis. Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition associated with this patient's symptoms?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician by his parents due to the appearance of purplish spots on his legs for the past 3 days and tea-colored urine for the past week. He also mentions having experienced mild abdominal pain and joint aches in his knees and ankles. The patient's past medical history is unremarkable, and he is up-to-date on his vaccinations. His vital signs are stable. On examination, there is palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Blood tests show normal platelet count and renal function. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man visits the clinic due to painful, swollen fingers and toes over the past three months. He also reports skin changes, including red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees that have persisted for several years. He denies morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. His family history is significant for his mother having a similar skin condition. On examination, there is asymmetric swelling of the distal interphalangeal joints and dactylitis. X-rays of the hands reveal pencil-in-cup deformity of some of the affected joints. Laboratory tests show normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and sleep disturbances for the past 6 months. He reports feeling tired even after a full night's sleep and mentions that stress at work has worsened his symptoms. He denies fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness over multiple muscle groups but no synovitis or joint swelling. Laboratory tests, including complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and thyroid function tests, are normal. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and a rash that has developed over the past week. He reports being bitten by a tick about three weeks ago while hiking in a wooded area. Initially, he experienced flu-like symptoms, but now he is more concerned about his left knee, which is swollen and painful. On examination, there is a circular, erythematous rash with central clearing on his right forearm. He denies any recent travel, gastrointestinal symptoms, or urinary problems. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with generalized body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He reports that he had a viral infection with fever and sore throat approximately three weeks ago, which resolved without treatment. Since then, he has been experiencing diffuse muscle and joint pain, predominantly in the mornings, which improves slightly as the day progresses. He denies any significant swelling of the joints. He has no significant medical history and is not on any regular medications. Vital signs reveal a temperature of 98.6°F, blood pressure of 120/80 mmHg, and a heart rate of 80 beats per minute. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in creatine kinase and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man visits the dermatology clinic with complaints of a rash and joint pain. He notes that the rash began on his face after a weekend of hiking. The rash is described as red and covers his cheeks and the bridge of his nose, sparing the nasolabial folds. He also reports pain in his wrists and knees, accompanied by morning stiffness lasting about an hour. Review of systems reveals oral ulcers and significant fatigue. He denies any recent infections, fever, or weight changes. His family history is significant for autoimmune diseases. Vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, the facial rash is noted along with mild tenderness in the wrist and knee joints without swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) and mild anemia. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of persistent skin lesions and joint pain. He reports having red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees for the past several months, which have recently started to itch. Additionally, he has noticed swelling and pain in his fingers, particularly on the right hand, which are worse in the morning and improve throughout the day. He denies any significant morning stiffness beyond 30 minutes and has no history of trauma. His family history is notable for a father with psoriasis. On examination, erythematous plaques covered with silvery scales are observed on the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees. The distal interphalangeal joints of the right hand show asymmetrical swelling and tenderness. Laboratory studies reveal normal rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the last 3 months. He reports weakness in his legs that has made climbing stairs increasingly challenging. He also mentions occasional tingling in his feet and lower legs. His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent infections or injuries. On physical examination, he exhibits decreased strength in both lower extremities, more pronounced proximally. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities, and his gait is unsteady. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the clinic complaining of persistent fatigue, generalized joint pain, and easy bruising over the past three months. He reports occasional fevers and night sweats. On examination, he appears pale, and there are petechiae noted on his lower limbs. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9.2 g/dL, a white blood cell count of 2,300/mm³, and a platelet count of 70,000/mm³. Peripheral blood smear shows normocytic anemia, and a bone marrow biopsy is performed revealing hypocellular marrow with marked reduction in hematopoietic cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that developed over the past two weeks. He reports a recent hiking trip where he recalls removing a tick from his upper arm. The patient states that the joint pain initially started in his knees and has now progressed to his wrists and elbows. He also noticed the development of a circular rash with a clear center on his torso. He has been experiencing fatigue and mild headaches. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, he has a temperature of 100.4°F (38°C), blood pressure of 118/76 mmHg, and a rash consistent with erythema migrans. Joint examination reveals tenderness and mild swelling in the knees and wrists. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with difficulty walking, which he has noticed progressively worsening over the past 2 weeks. He describes a feeling of weakness in his right foot, causing him to trip frequently. He denies any recent trauma. His medical history includes hypertension, managed with lisinopril. He works as a carpenter and recently started a new project that requires climbing ladders frequently. On examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and diminished sensation over the dorsum of the right foot. Reflexes are 2+ and symmetrical in the upper extremities and left lower extremity but diminished at the right ankle. The remainder of the neurological examination is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy box at home. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-thoracic region. He denies any trauma, but states that he has had similar, albeit milder, episodes in the past. His medical history is notable for hypertension, type II diabetes mellitus, and chronic glucocorticoid use for polymyalgia rheumatica. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. His temperature is 98.4°F (36.9°C), blood pressure is 145/85 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the thoracic spine with limited range of motion due to pain. Laboratory studies show normal calcium and phosphate levels. A plain radiograph of the spine reveals a compressed vertebral body. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old Caucasian male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain, fatigue, and a rash that appeared after he returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States. He recalls being bitten by a tick but did not seek medical attention initially. The rash started on his back and has a targetoid appearance. He also reports recent episodes of fever and malaise. On examination, he has tenderness and swelling in the knees and wrists. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive IgM antibody for Borrelia burgdorferi. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of recent onset purpura on his lower extremities and dark-colored urine over the past week. He also mentions experiencing mild joint pain and fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, he is afebrile, his blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 76/min, and respirations are 18/min. Laboratory studies reveal hematuria, proteinuria, and elevated creatinine levels. Blood tests show a mildly elevated ESR and C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and a noticeable slapping gait. He reports that his foot seems to drag, especially when he is not paying attention. He also mentions mild numbness and a tingling sensation in the distal part of the left leg. His medical history is significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. He does not smoke and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion of the left foot, decreased sensation over the dorsum of the left foot and anterior lateral leg, and a steppage gait. There is no significant swelling or tenderness in any of the joints. Laboratory studies show normal inflammatory markers including erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 49-year-old man presents to the clinic with persistent neck pain and tingling sensations in his right arm for the past several months. He reports that the pain is worse with neck movements and sometimes radiates down into his hand. He denies any recent trauma or illness. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is reduced range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of his right forearm and thumb. Muscle strength in the right upper extremity is slightly reduced compared to the left. His laboratory results show normal complete blood count, normal renal function, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 28 mm/h. A cervical spine X-ray reveals degenerative changes but no acute fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 2-year history of recurrent episodes of painful oral ulcers. He also reports experiencing painful genital ulcers over the past year. The lesions typically heal over a few weeks, but have been recurrent. Recently, he has had joint pain in his knees and elbows and complains of fatigue. He denies any gastrointestinal symptoms or recent travel history. On examination, there are several aphthous ulcers in the oral cavity, and tender, erythematous nodules are noted on the shins. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a history of chronic pain and stiffness in both hands and wrists that has been worsening over the past year. He reports that the pain is most severe in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. He also notes the presence of firm, non-tender nodules on his elbows. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive rheumatoid factor. Radiographs of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Based on these findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past year. He also notes occasional swelling of the area around his jaws which he describes as uncomfortable but not painful. He mentions feeling more fatigued than usual, and he has some difficulty swallowing dry foods. He has a past medical history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, the physician observes bilateral parotid gland enlargement and conjunctival dryness. Laboratory tests reveal a slightly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) but normal rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and painful, firm nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms and elbows. He notes that the nodules have been gradually increasing in size and number. He also reports occasional fevers and significant weight loss over the last two months. He denies recent travel, new medications, or allergies. His medical history is significant for poorly controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. He works as a mechanic and has smoked one pack of cigarettes daily for 25 years. His temperature is 100.2°F (37.9°C), blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, pulse is 85/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces and mild tenderness in several joints of the hands. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of hand pain and stiffness that have progressively worsened over the past six months. He notes that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and often lasts for more than an hour. He reports difficulty in performing daily activities such as opening jars and turning doorknobs. On examination, the metacarpophalangeal joints and proximal interphalangeal joints of both hands are swollen and tender. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. The erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is also elevated. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 34-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches, fatigue, and mild fever for the past two weeks. He mentions that these symptoms started after a recent upper respiratory infection. He denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness but feels generally unwell and has difficulty performing his usual activities. Physical examination reveals tender points over several muscle groups but no synovitis or joint deformities. Laboratory studies show elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) and normal rheumatoid factor (RF) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a complaint of a blotchy, reddish-purple discoloration on his legs that he has noticed over the past two weeks. He reports a history of recurrent pain and swelling in his calves, which has been happening intermittently for several months. The patient states that he experienced a similar discoloration during an episode of unexplained abdominal pain a few months ago. He also mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue. His past medical history is significant for frequent headaches, treated with over-the-counter pain medications. His temperature is 37°C (98.6°F), pulse is 82/min, respirations are 14/min, and blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on both lower extremities and tenderness in the calves. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and electrolytes but an elevated D-dimer level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care office with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations radiating down his right arm. He mentions that the symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past six months. He notes that the pain is more pronounced in the morning and tends to improve slightly as the day progresses. He has a history of hypertension and takes lisinopril. He denies any recent trauma. On physical exam, there is reduced sensation in the lateral aspect of the forearm and thumb, with diminished reflexes in the right biceps. Cervical spine x-rays show degenerative changes. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 72/min, and respirations are 16/min. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking over the past 3 months. He reports increasing weakness in his legs, which he first noticed when he had trouble climbing stairs. He also notes occasional tingling in his feet and has developed fatigue and joint stiffness in the mornings that last for about an hour. He denies any recent infections, trauma, or weight loss. On examination, there is weakness in the lower extremities, hyperreflexia, and a positive Babinski sign. Laboratory tests show a mild elevation in inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. MRI of the brain and spinal cord demonstrates lesions in the white matter. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with a 6-month history of skin tightening in his fingers. He also reports episodes of finger discoloration upon exposure to cold, initially turning white, then blue, and finally red upon warming. He experiences occasional joint pain and fatigue but does not have morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. His family history is unremarkable, and he works as an office manager. Physical examination reveals shiny, thickened skin over the fingers and hands with pitting scars on the fingertips. Capillaroscopy shows dilated capillary loops. Laboratory tests are pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent nasal congestion, intermittent nosebleeds, and increasing fatigue. He also reports having a cough with occasional blood-tinged sputum and has experienced some difficulty breathing over the past few weeks. His medical history is significant for hypertension, and he has smoked half a pack of cigarettes per day for 20 years. On examination, he appears pale and has a mild fever. Lung auscultation reveals crackles at the lung bases. Laboratory studies show:

Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL
Leukocyte count: 9,200/mm3
Creatinine: 3.5 mg/dL
Urinalysis: 3+ protein, 3+ blood

CT of the chest reveals bilateral cavitary lung nodules. A renal biopsy shows glomerulonephritis with necrotizing features and no immune deposits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic due to chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has been progressively worsening over the past year. He reports that the pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. He denies any recent trauma, weight loss, or fever. His medical history is unremarkable, except for occasional episodes of mild knee pain. He is a non-smoker and works as a software engineer. On physical examination, there is reduced flexion of the lumbar spine, and the Schober test is positive. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels but are negative for rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue, joint stiffness, and a chronic history of sinus infections. Recently, he has noticed increased shortness of breath and a persistent cough. He denies tobacco use and has no significant past medical history other than recurrent sinusitis. On examination, his blood pressure is 150/95 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Lung auscultation reveals bilateral crackles. Laboratory results show elevated creatinine levels and hematuria. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Considering the constellation of symptoms, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of chronic lower back pain. He describes the pain as more pronounced in the morning, with stiffness continuing for over an hour, and some improvement with activity as the day progresses. His medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent trauma or injury. On physical examination, there is limited spinal mobility, particularly affecting the lumbar region. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). HLA-B27 antigen testing is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive muscle weakness over the past few months. He notes difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. He also mentions some joint pain and morning stiffness that improves throughout the day. He denies any recent trauma or changes in physical activity. Past medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. His temperature is 98.6°F (37.0°C), blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, pulse is 82/min, and respirations are 14/min. Laboratory tests reveal elevated muscle enzymes and a mild elevation in C-reactive protein. An electromyography study is performed and shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive joint deformities in his hands and wrists over the past year. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and difficulty performing daily tasks such as buttoning his shirt. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally with ulnar deviation. Laboratory studies reveal elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows juxta-articular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of acute pain and swelling in his right great toe over the past year. He describes sudden onset of severe pain, redness, and warmth, usually lasting for a few days, which then resolves spontaneously. He reports similar episodes occurring in the knees and elbows. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He drinks 3-4 beers daily and has a family history of arthritis. On examination, there are firm subcutaneous nodules palpated over the elbows and helix of the ear. Joint aspiration of the affected toe reveals needle-shaped, negatively birefringent crystals. Laboratory tests show: 
Serum uric acid 9.2 mg/dL (N = 3.5-7.2)
C-reactive protein (CRP) 15 mg/L (N < 10)
Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling sensations radiating down his right arm for the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache, occasionally sharp, particularly when turning his head to the right. There are no reports of trauma. He experiences occasional weakness in his grip strength on the right hand. His medical history is notable for minor osteoarthritis in his knees. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the neck and diminished sensation in the right C6 dermatome. X-ray of the cervical spine shows some degenerative changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the rheumatology clinic with progressive joint deformity in his hands and wrists over the past two years. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour and difficulty performing daily activities due to pain and swelling in the joints. His medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints, with noticeable ulnar deviation. Laboratory tests show a positive rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies. An x-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the neurologist with progressive difficulty walking over the past 6 months. He describes weakness in his lower extremities, with increased fatigue after walking short distances. He experiences occasional numbness and tingling in both legs and has had a few falls recently. His medical history is unremarkable except for mild hypertension treated with lisinopril. On examination, he has increased tone in the lower extremities, hyperreflexia, and Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Sensory examination reveals a slight decrease in proprioception and vibration sense in the feet. MRI of the spine shows degeneration of the lateral columns. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old man comes to the clinic reporting progressive muscle weakness over the past two months, particularly affecting his shoulders and hips. He describes difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. He denies any joint pain, morning stiffness, or swelling. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes and hypertension, both well-controlled. On examination, he has symmetric proximal muscle weakness, but no visible muscle atrophy. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated creatine kinase (CK) level and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Electromyography shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive weakness, primarily affecting his upper arms and thighs. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead. The patient denies any joint pain, swelling, or morning stiffness. He has a past medical history of hypertension, managed with lisinopril. He denies alcohol use and smokes occasionally. On physical examination, there is symmetric muscle weakness in the proximal muscles, with preserved distal strength. There is no rash or skin changes. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain. He reports that the nodules appeared two weeks ago and have been accompanied by a dull ache in his knees and ankles. He notes that the joint pain is worse in the morning and often makes it difficult for him to get out of bed. He denies any recent infections, fever, weight loss, or travel history. On physical examination, there are multiple tender, erythematous nodules over his anterior lower legs. Laboratory results show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Chest X-ray is notable for bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of a rash on his face that worsens after sun exposure. He describes joint pain in both his wrists and knees that has persisted for the last few months. He also reports experiencing fatigue and mouth sores. He has no significant past medical history and is not currently taking any medications. Physical examination reveals a butterfly-shaped erythematous rash over the cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory tests show a positive ANA (antinuclear antibody) and elevated ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of skin changes in his hands. He reports progressive tightening of the skin over his fingers and recurrent episodes of fingers turning white and painful in cold weather, followed by a bluish color, and finally reddening upon rewarming. He also mentions joint pain and occasional swelling of his hands, as well as fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable. On physical examination, there is noted thickening and tightening of the skin over the fingers, and capillary nailfold microscopy shows dilation and dropout of capillaries. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) with a nucleolar pattern, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to his neurologist with a 6-month history of progressive difficulty walking due to lower extremity weakness. He reports frequent falls and notes that climbing stairs has become particularly challenging. The patient also mentions intermittent tingling in his feet and occasional bladder incontinence. His medical history is significant for a past episode of optic neuritis two years ago, which resolved without intervention. Neurological examination reveals spastic paraparesis and hyperreflexia in the lower extremities. An MRI of the brain and spinal cord shows multiple hyperintense lesions on T2-weighted images, some of which appear to have enhanced with gadolinium. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 70-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden onset of severe back pain. He describes the pain as sharp and located in the mid-thoracic region. He denies any trauma or fall. His medical history is significant for chronic glucocorticoid use for polymyalgia rheumatica. He also reports long-standing morning stiffness and generalized joint pain for the past few years. He does not smoke or consume alcohol. On examination, he is tender to palpation over the thoracic spine. Neurological examination is normal. X-rays of the spine reveal a compression fracture of the T8 vertebra. His laboratory workup shows normal calcium and phosphorus levels, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely underlying condition leading to his current presentation?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with recurrent episodes of acute pain and swelling in his right big toe over the past year. The pain is sudden in onset and often wakes him from sleep at night. He reports that the toe becomes red, warm, and tender during these episodes, and they usually resolve within a week. He has noticed small, hard nodules in the skin around his toe and elbow. He drinks alcohol frequently and has a family history of arthritis. His physical examination reveals tenderness and erythema over the first metatarsophalangeal joint of the right foot and the presence of several subcutaneous nodules. Laboratory tests show elevated serum uric acid levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a 2-week history of a rash on his legs and blood in his urine. The parents report that the rash appeared after a recent upper respiratory infection and has been persistent, despite the use of over-the-counter antihistamines. On examination, the rash is identified as palpable purpura distributed over the buttocks and lower extremities. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and mild proteinuria. The patient also complains of abdominal pain and swelling in his knees. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a primary complaint of bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a deep, aching sensation that is aggravated by prolonged standing and walking. He denies any significant morning stiffness, but notes that the pain improves slightly with rest. He works as a warehouse manager, which involves considerable physical activity. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. On physical examination, there is limited range of motion in both hips, with pain on internal rotation. There is no swelling or erythema over the joints. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers, including erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent swelling in his neck and armpits, accompanied by night sweats, unintentional weight loss, and generalized fatigue. He reports a low-grade fever but denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional sinus infections. On physical examination, there is palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests reveal elevated lactate dehydrogenase and mild anemia. A biopsy of one of the enlarged lymph nodes is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has been worsening over the past year. The pain is particularly severe in the morning and improves with activity throughout the day. The patient works as a software engineer and denies any recent trauma or overexertion. He reports occasional fatigue but denies any significant weight loss, fever, or joint swelling. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility, particularly in the lumbar region, and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory studies are notable for elevated inflammatory markers. Radiographic imaging of the sacroiliac joints shows signs of inflammation and sclerosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old male comes to the clinic due to persistent nasal congestion and cough for the past 3 months. He reports occasional hemoptysis and fatigue. Over the last month, he has also noticed swelling in his legs and decreased urine output. He has a history of hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. Physical examination reveals bilateral crackles in the lungs and 2+ pitting edema in both legs. Laboratory tests show elevated creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. A chest X-ray shows multiple bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. A urinalysis reveals hematuria, proteinuria, and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in his feet and hands. He reports difficulty with buttoning his shirt and frequently dropping objects. The symptoms are gradually worsening. He denies any recent trauma, alcohol use, or new medications. His past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 8 years ago, which is poorly controlled, and hyperlipidemia. He is currently on metformin and atorvastatin. On examination, there is mild weakness in the intrinsic muscles of the hands and decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory studies show normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 6 months. He also notes swelling in the areas around his jaw over the past few weeks. He reports feeling fatigued but denies joint pain or swelling. His past medical history is notable for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On examination, there is bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory studies reveal positive anti-SSA/Ro antibodies and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old male presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent attacks of severe pain and swelling in his right great toe. He reports that the episodes occur suddenly, often waking him from sleep, and have happened three times in the past year. He describes the pain as so intense that even the weight of a bedsheet is unbearable. He has noticed a firm, yellowish-white nodule on the same toe. His medical history is notable for obesity and hypertension, and he admits to consuming alcohol regularly. Laboratory results show an elevated serum uric acid level. Joint aspiration reveals negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals under polarized light. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with generalized body aches and persistent fatigue that started a week after recovering from a mild upper respiratory tract infection. He describes the pain as diffuse and involving his muscles more than his joints. He denies any fever, skin rashes, or recent weight loss. His medical history is unremarkable aside from seasonal allergies. Physical examination reveals mild tenderness in the muscles of the upper arms and thighs, but no significant joint swelling or redness. Laboratory tests show mildly elevated creatine kinase levels and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's current symptoms?

A 35-year-old man visits his physician due to joint pain and swelling for the past several months. He reports that the discomfort began in several fingers and toes but has now also affected his knees and ankles. He has noticed that his joints are more stiff after inactivity, particularly in the morning. Additionally, he mentions having red, scaly patches on his scalp and elbows. His family history includes a father with similar skin lesions. On examination, there is swelling in the distal interphalangeal joints and dactylitis in several toes. Skin examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales. Laboratory tests show mild elevation in ESR and CRP, while rheumatoid factor is negative. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of tightness in his fingers and color changes in his hands that have been occurring over the past few months. He notices that his fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red upon warming after exposure to cold. He also experiences occasional joint pain and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. He reports difficulty in opening jars and handling small objects due to the skin changes. Physical examination reveals thickened, shiny skin over the fingers and hands. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 60-year-old Caucasian male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain in his hands and wrists for the past four months. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and lasts for over an hour. He denies any recent illnesses or infections. His medical history is significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 10.5 g/dL, leukocyte count of 7000/mm3, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and rheumatoid factor is positive. An x-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain that has migrated from his knees to his wrists over the past week. He reports a sore throat that resolved spontaneously about two weeks ago. He denies fever, rash, or weight loss. On examination, he has tenderness and mild swelling in multiple joints, with no visible erythema. His vital signs are within normal limits, and there is no evidence of any rash or subcutaneous nodules. Laboratory workup reveals an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). His antistreptolysin O (ASO) titer is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the emergency department with recurrent fevers, night sweats, and swollen lymph nodes for the past two weeks. He denies any significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, there are palpable lymph nodes in the cervical and axillary regions. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. The patient reports recently starting a new job as an elementary school teacher. He has no recent travel history or known sick contacts. A chest x-ray shows mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue, low-grade fever, and weight loss. He also reports multiple firm, painless subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and knees that have developed over the past few weeks. He has a history of hypertension managed with lisinopril. He denies any recent infections, new medications, or travel. On examination, he appears tired and has multiple subcutaneous nodules on extensor surfaces. Joint examination shows mild swelling and tenderness in both wrists. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with a negative rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary nodules. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of right shoulder pain. He describes the pain as a dull ache that has progressively worsened, limiting his ability to reach overhead or lift heavy objects. He reports no trauma to the shoulder. He works as an office manager and spends long hours at a desk. His medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes, which is well-controlled with metformin. On physical examination, there is restricted active and passive range of motion in the right shoulder, with noticeable discomfort during abduction and external rotation. There is no swelling or erythema. The remainder of the musculoskeletal exam is unremarkable. Laboratory tests show a normal white blood cell count, mildly elevated C-reactive protein (CRP), and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling that started the morning after playing a vigorous game of basketball. He denies any direct trauma to the knee during the game. The swelling has progressively worsened, and he reports difficulty walking due to the pain. He denies similar episodes in the past and has no significant medical history. On examination, the right knee is warm, swollen, and tender with decreased range of motion. The patient's temperature is 99.1°F (37.3°C). Laboratory tests show a white blood cell count of 12,000/mm³ with a high percentage of neutrophils. An X-ray of the knee shows soft tissue swelling without any fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive difficulty climbing stairs and lifting heavy objects over the past three months. He denies any joint pain or swelling but mentions occasional fatigue. The patient has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On examination, he has notable weakness in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs, while distal muscle strength remains intact. Sensation and reflexes are normal. Laboratory studies show an elevated serum creatine kinase level. Electromyography reveals myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of dry eyes and dry mouth. He also reports mild joint pain and fatigue. On examination, there is noticeable enlargement of the parotid glands bilaterally. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Laboratory studies show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with a two-month history of painless, firm nodules on his elbows and forearms. He reports experiencing fatigue, low-grade fevers, and a 10-pound weight loss over the same period. He also mentions occasional joint discomfort, particularly in the mornings. His past medical history is notable for a long-standing history of smoking. Physical examination reveals multiple subcutaneous nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms. Laboratory evaluation shows a mild anemia and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute knee pain and swelling following a weekend basketball match. He reports twisting his knee during a play and felt a 'pop' at the time of injury. Since then, he has experienced increasing pain and difficulty bearing weight on the affected leg. He denies any history of prior knee issues and has no significant past medical history. Physical examination reveals a swollen right knee with reduced range of motion. There is tenderness along the medial joint line and a positive McMurray test. An X-ray of the knee is unremarkable. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man visits the neurology clinic due to tingling and numbness in his hands and feet for the past 6 months. He reports that the sensation is worse at night and occasionally wakes him from sleep. He also mentions having some joint pain and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes, although these symptoms are less bothersome. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, which has been poorly controlled. Laboratory tests reveal elevated fasting blood glucose levels and a hemoglobin A1c of 9.1%. Nerve conduction studies indicate decreased conduction velocity in the peripheral nerves. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for this patient's peripheral neuropathy?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with right shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. He describes a dull aching sensation with occasional sharp pains and difficulty reaching overhead. He denies any recent trauma or overuse. His medical history is significant for well-controlled type 2 diabetes and mild hyperlipidemia. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anterior shoulder with a markedly limited range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. The patient has a normal temperature, heart rate, and blood pressure. X-ray of the shoulder reveals no fractures or dislocations, but there is some narrowing of the joint space. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive weakness in his shoulders and hips. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects overhead, as well as fatigue, but denies any joint pain, fever, or weight loss. The patient has a history of well-controlled hypertension and is an active smoker. Physical examination reveals symmetric proximal muscle weakness and diminished muscle bulk, with no tender or swollen joints. Laboratory tests show an elevated creatine kinase level. Considering this presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 5-month history of chronic fatigue and weight gain. He also mentions that he has been experiencing joint stiffness in the morning, which lasts for about 30 minutes and improves throughout the day. He denies any significant medical history, although he mentions that his father had a “thyroid problem”. On examination, he appears overweight, with dry skin and coarse hair. His vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal the following:
TSH: 12.0 mIU/L (normal 0.4-4.0 mIU/L)
Free T4: 0.6 ng/dL (normal 0.7-1.9 ng/dL)
Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 24-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash on his face and joint pain in his hands and knees. He notes that the rash appears to worsen with sun exposure. He also reports experiencing fatigue and has had several painful ulcers in his mouth over the past few weeks. He denies any recent illness but mentions that his mother has a history of autoimmune disease. On examination, he has a malar rash and mild synovitis in the small joints of his hands. Laboratory tests reveal anemia, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), and the presence of antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of burning sensations and tingling in his feet over the past few months. He describes the sensation as worsening at night and occasionally affecting his hands. The patient denies any significant joint pain or swelling but notes occasional fatigue and morning stiffness that lasts less than 30 minutes. He has a history of type 2 diabetes, which is poorly controlled, with an HbA1c of 9.2%. On physical exam, there is decreased sensation to pinprick and vibration in a stocking-glove distribution. Laboratory tests reveal elevated blood glucose and normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 59-year-old male presents to the emergency department with severe pain in his right wrist following a fall onto an outstretched hand. He was walking his dog in the park when he tripped over a curb and landed on his right hand. He describes the pain as sharp and constant, and it worsens with movement. On physical examination, there is marked swelling and tenderness over the distal radius, with limited range of motion. An X-ray of the wrist shows a dorsal displacement of the distal radius. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial management for this patient's condition?

options.B: Apply a splint and refer for orthopedic evaluation
options.D: Order MRI to assess for ligamentous injury
options.G: Perform immediate open reduction and internal fixation

question: A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall on an outstretched hand while playing soccer. He reports immediate pain and swelling and is unable to move his wrist without significant discomfort. He denies any previous wrist injuries or chronic joint issues. His medical history is notable for well-controlled hypertension and occasional low back pain. Physical examination reveals swelling, tenderness, and limited range of motion of the right wrist. An x-ray of the wrist is performed (Figure A). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a persistent fever of 101°F (38.3°C) for the past three weeks. He reports feeling fatigued and experiencing night sweats. He denies weight loss, cough, or any recent travel. On examination, he appears uncomfortable but is in no acute distress. His heart rate is 92 beats per minute, and his blood pressure is 120/80 mmHg. Laboratory findings reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures drawn on admission are pending. Chest X-ray is normal, and abdominal ultrasound shows no significant findings. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.C: Conduct further imaging to evaluate for occult abscess

question: A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in his lower extremities. He notes that over the past three months, he has developed increasing fatigue and occasional tingling in his feet. He denies any recent trauma, back pain, or changes in bowel or bladder function. His medical history includes well-controlled hypertension and a previous smoking history of 30 pack-years. He currently takes lisinopril. On examination, his vital signs are within normal limits. Neurologic examination reveals weakness in the hip flexors and knee extensors bilaterally, with decreased deep tendon reflexes in the lower limbs. Sensory examination shows reduced vibration sense in the feet. Laboratory findings show a mild normocytic anemia, elevated creatine kinase, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate of 55 mm/hr. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 6-month history of fatigue and morning stiffness lasting more than an hour. He reports joint pain affecting both his wrists and knees. His past medical history includes managed hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He denies any recent infections or trauma. On physical exam, there is swelling and tenderness in multiple joints of the hands and knees. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 90 fL, erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. An X-ray of the hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 67-year-old man presents to the clinic with sudden onset of severe back pain after lifting a heavy object. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the mid-lower back, which worsens with movement and coughing. The patient has a medical history significant for chronic corticosteroid use due to a previous diagnosis of polymyalgia rheumatica. Physical examination reveals tenderness to palpation over the T12-L1 vertebrae, with no neurological deficits observed. His laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but his complete blood count and metabolic panel are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 34-year-old male presents to the emergency department with acute right knee pain and swelling that started after he finished a basketball game. He recalls twisting his knee awkwardly during a jump, followed by immediate pain and swelling. He denies any previous injuries to this knee. He has a history of asthma and seasonal allergies for which he uses an inhaler as needed. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. Physical examination reveals a moderate effusion in the right knee and tenderness along the joint line. The knee is warm to the touch with limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the knee shows no fractures. An MRI is ordered, and it reveals a tear in the anterior cruciate ligament (ACL). Which of the following is the next best step in management?

A 24-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a 6-month history of chronic back pain. The pain is more pronounced in the morning and improves with activity. He reports occasional episodes of blurry vision and recent fatigue, but denies any bowel or urinary symptoms. On physical examination, the patient has a decreased range of motion in his lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. His laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers. He has no family history of autoimmune diseases. What is the most likely diagnosis in this patient?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with acute onset of severe lower back pain after bending down to pick up a newspaper. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the midline of his lower back. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or leg weakness. His past medical history is notable for osteoporosis and hypertension, for which he takes alendronate and lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness over the L1 vertebra and limited range of motion due to pain, but no neurological deficits. Laboratory tests show normal calcium and phosphate levels. An X-ray of the lumbar spine reveals a compression fracture of the L1 vertebra. What is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a deep ache that is exacerbated by activity and relieved somewhat by rest. His medical history includes smoking, controlled hypertension, and hyperlipidemia. He denies any morning stiffness or swelling in other joints but mentions occasional low back pain. On physical examination, there is tenderness on palpation of the hips and limited range of motion, particularly with internal rotation. Laboratory tests show normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). X-rays of the pelvis reveal joint space narrowing and subchondral sclerosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue over the past 8 months. He notes increased difficulty in concentrating at work and has also experienced a 5 kg weight gain. He reports joint pain, particularly in the morning, which improves as the day progresses. Laboratory tests reveal a low free thyroxine (T4) level and an elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level. He denies any recent illness, fevers, or night sweats. His family history is significant for autoimmune thyroid disease. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old male presents to the urgent care clinic with acute onset of pain and swelling in his right knee after playing a weekend soccer match. He reports that the knee feels stiff and he has difficulty bearing weight on the affected leg. He denies any recent trauma or history of joint issues. On examination, the right knee is swollen, warm, and tender to touch, with limited range of motion. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and ESR. An arthrocentesis is performed, and the synovial fluid is yellow and cloudy with an elevated white blood cell count. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of recurrent painful mouth ulcers and genital sores for the past several months. He also mentions occasional joint pain, particularly in his knees and ankles, which improves with over-the-counter analgesics. He denies any significant past medical history and reports no recent travel or new sexual partners. Upon examination, there are multiple shallow ulcers in the oral cavity and two ulcerated lesions in the genital area. His eyes are mildly red, and he reports occasional blurry vision. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers but negative results for rheumatoid factor and antinuclear antibodies. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with a two-week history of diffuse body aches and fatigue following a mild viral upper respiratory infection. He describes the pain as aching and non-specific, affecting multiple areas including his shoulders, back, and thighs. He denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. The patient reports feeling generally unwell and has noticed occasional low-grade fevers. On examination, he appears tired but has no joint tenderness or swelling. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count, but elevated inflammatory markers such as C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of fatigue and joint pain. He reports stiffness in his hands and wrists that is most pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour. He also notes swelling and pain in the same joints. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes lisinopril and atorvastatin. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history but quit 5 years ago. On examination, there is synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin level of 11.2 g/dL, a mean corpuscular volume of 85 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 65 mm/hr. Rheumatoid factor is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive weakness primarily affecting his shoulders and thighs. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. Additionally, he has noticed some joint pain and fatigue but denies any morning stiffness or swelling in joints. His physical examination shows 3/5 strength in proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs, while distal muscle strength is normal. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and mild elevation of inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic complaining of a rash and joint pain for the past two weeks. He notes the development of purple spots on his legs and lower back pain. He also reports dark, tea-colored urine intermittently over the last few days. He denies any recent infections, medication changes, or travel history. On physical examination, palpable purpura is noted on the lower extremities. Laboratory findings reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. His blood pressure is 135/85 mm Hg. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to joint pain that has been migrating over the past two weeks. She mentions that he had a sore throat about three weeks ago, which she attributed to a common cold. The boy reports that the joint pain started in his knees and then moved to his elbows and wrists, but it seems to resolve in one joint as it appears in another. He also mentions feeling more tired than usual. Physical examination reveals a low-grade fever and mild swelling of the left wrist. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of persistent bone pain and fatigue for the past several months. He has also noticed an increase in thirst and has been urinating more frequently. His medical history is unremarkable, and he reports no recent injuries. He denies tobacco use, but he drinks alcohol occasionally on weekends. A physical examination is notable for generalized bone tenderness, especially in the lower back and pelvis. Laboratory studies reveal hypercalcemia, elevated alkaline phosphatase, and normal renal function. A bone scan shows diffuse increased uptake in the axial skeleton. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his family physician with a complaint of persistent fatigue over the past several months. He mentions experiencing joint pain, especially in his wrists and knees, which seems to worsen in the morning but improves throughout the day. He has also noticed some unintentional weight loss and feels more sensitive to cold temperatures recently. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he has no known allergies. On examination, the patient appears pale, with dry skin and a slightly enlarged thyroid gland. Laboratory tests reveal low serum T4, elevated TSH levels, and a positive anti-thyroid peroxidase antibody. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent deep vein thromboses and a rash on his lower extremities that he describes as a purplish, net-like pattern. He has no significant past medical history of autoimmune disease, but he reports occasional joint pain and swelling in his hands and knees. He denies any recent trauma or infections. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the legs and normal joint range of motion with mild tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a platelet count of 150,000/mm3, partial thromboplastin time (PTT) of 40 seconds, and positive lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while hiking. He reports immediate swelling and an inability to move the wrist without significant pain. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and bruising is visible. He denies any history of similar injuries or chronic joint issues, though he does mention occasional morning stiffness in his fingers that usually resolves within an hour. An x-ray of the wrist is performed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old male presents to the clinic with fatigue and significant weight loss over the past 3 months. He reports feeling tired during the day despite adequate sleep and has unintentionally lost 15 pounds. He also notes occasional joint discomfort but no significant morning stiffness. His medical history includes hypertension, controlled with medication. On examination, he appears pale and has mild hepatosplenomegaly. There is no lymphadenopathy or skin rash. Laboratory studies reveal:

Hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL
Hematocrit 28%
WBC count 5,200/mm3
Platelet count 150,000/mm3
Serum iron 20 µg/dL
