Total iron binding capacity 250 µg/dL
Ferritin 900 ng/mL
ESR 35 mm/hr

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of recurrent fevers and generalized fatigue. He reports experiencing night sweats and unintentional weight loss over the same period. He also mentions occasional joint pain in his knees and wrists, which is worse in the morning but improves throughout the day. On examination, there is notable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. His complete blood count shows anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. A chest radiograph reveals mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his mother due to a rash that developed over the past week. She notes that the rash first appeared on his lower legs and has now spread to his thighs. Additionally, he has been complaining of dark-colored urine and mild joint pain. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, he has palpable purpura on his buttocks and legs. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine, hematuria, and proteinuria. A renal biopsy shows mesangial deposition of IgA. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with fatigue, weight loss, and fever over the past three months. He reports the development of small, painless nodules on the extensor surfaces of his forearms and elbows. He has a history of smoking and denies any recent infections or trauma. Physical examination reveals firm, movable subcutaneous nodules and mild tenderness in the wrist joints. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and C-reactive protein levels. A biopsy of one of the nodules shows necrotic granulomas. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old man comes to the emergency department due to a rash and joint pain for the past week. He reports that the rash appeared first on his lower legs and consists of red-purple spots that do not blanche under pressure. He also notes experiencing mild joint pain and swelling, primarily affecting his knees and ankles. In addition, he has noticed blood in his urine. He denies any recent illness, travel, or new medications. His medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination shows palpable purpura on the lower extremities. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 3-month history of progressive proximal muscle weakness and difficulty climbing stairs. He also notes general fatigue and has had a 5-kg (11-lb) weight loss over this period. His past medical history is unremarkable. On examination, he has difficulty arising from a seated position without support. Neurological examination is normal except for the noted muscle weakness. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated creatine kinase (CK) level. An electromyography (EMG) study shows evidence of muscle membrane irritability. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with a 4-month history of progressive weight gain, particularly around the abdomen and face, and periorbital edema. He also reports morning joint stiffness lasting over an hour, fatigue, and occasional muscle weakness. His medical history includes hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication. On examination, he is found to have facial puffiness, mild pitting edema of the lower extremities, and non-tender swollen wrists. Laboratory tests reveal normal complete blood count, mildly elevated inflammatory markers, and a low serum albumin. Serum creatinine is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 64-year-old man comes to the clinic with a complaint of bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the last six months. The pain is described as a dull ache that sometimes radiates to the groin and thighs, and it is present at rest but worsens with activity. The patient notes stiffness in the morning that resolves within 30 minutes and denies any significant swelling, redness, or warmth in the joints. He has no significant past medical history but mentions a family history of hip problems. The patient denies fever, weight loss, or recent trauma. On examination, there is decreased range of motion in both hips with tenderness upon palpation. Laboratory tests show mild elevation in inflammatory markers but anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody is negative. A pelvic X-ray shows joint space narrowing and sclerosis. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue and joint pain. He reports that for the past 6 months, he has been experiencing morning stiffness lasting about an hour, which improves as the day progresses. He also notes swelling and pain in his hands and wrists. His medical history is significant for hypertension, managed with lisinopril. On physical examination, there is symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.8 g/dL, an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 62 mm/hr, and a positive rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent dry eyes and dry mouth over the past several months. He notes that he has been needing to drink water frequently and uses eye drops multiple times a day. Additionally, he reports fatigue and transient joint pains affecting his hands and knees. On examination, there is noticeable bilateral parotid gland enlargement. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past three months. He reports that his joint pain is mainly in the wrists and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes. He has been feeling increasingly tired, with difficulty completing his usual daily activities. On examination, the patient appears pale, and petechiae are noted on his arms and legs. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9.2 g/dL, a white blood cell count of 2,500/mm³, and a platelet count of 45,000/mm³. His rheumatoid factor is negative, and no significant lymphadenopathy is observed. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 57-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of progressive difficulty walking over the past 6 months. He describes weakness in his legs, which has made it increasingly hard to climb stairs or stand up from a seated position. He denies any sensory changes, sphincter dysfunction, or pain. His medical history is significant for hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medications. He is a retired construction worker and denies smoking or alcohol use. On physical examination, there is noticeable weakness in the lower extremities, particularly in the proximal muscles. Reflexes are brisk, and there is no muscle atrophy. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels, and a muscle biopsy demonstrates endomysial inflammation and necrotic muscle fibers. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 25-year-old man comes to the clinic with recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital lesions. He reports that the oral ulcers have been occurring every few months for the past year, and the genital ulcers are similarly recurrent. He describes the ulcers as painful and taking weeks to heal. He also complains of joint pain in both his knees and fatigue. There is no significant past medical history, and he is not on any regular medications. On examination, multiple aphthous ulcers are noted on the buccal mucosa, and there are healing ulcers on the glans penis. There is no visible rash, and his joints appear normal but tender on palpation. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, negative rheumatoid factor, and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with a 6-month history of neck pain that radiates to his right arm accompanied by numbness and tingling in the fingers. The pain is exacerbated by looking up or turning his head to the right. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or weight loss. Physical examination reveals a positive Spurling's test and decreased strength in the right biceps. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished in the right biceps as well. Cervical spine X-rays show degenerative changes with osteophyte formation. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient?

options.B: Order an MRI of the cervical spine

question: A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of scaly, erythematous patches on his elbows and knees, accompanied by joint pain in his fingers and toes. He notes that his fingers appear swollen, and he experiences stiffness in the morning that improves as the day goes on. He denies any family history of arthritis but mentions a family history of skin conditions. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Rheumatoid factor is negative, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) is also negative. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and pencil-in-cup deformity of the distal interphalangeal joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling off his bicycle. He reports landing on his outstretched hand. The patient states that he experiences sharp pain when attempting to move his wrist, especially with gripping motions. He denies any other injuries and states that he has not noticed any swelling or loss of motion in other joints. There is no significant past medical history or family history of joint diseases. On physical examination, there is tenderness over the anatomical snuffbox and limited range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is ordered. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.C: Immobilization with a thumb spica splint

question: A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of a persistent rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure. He mentions that he also experiences joint pain, particularly in his hands and knees, which has been troubling him for the past few months. The patient denies any morning stiffness lasting longer than half an hour but notes that his pain is exacerbated after spending time outdoors. He has also noticed increased fatigue and some hair thinning. On examination, a malar rash that spares the nasolabial folds is observed. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain in his wrists and knees, and occasional fevers over the past two months. He works as a software engineer and has no significant past medical history. He denies any recent travel or infections. On examination, he appears pale and has mild tenderness in his wrists and knees without obvious swelling. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,800/mm³, and platelet count of 100,000/mm³. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated at 45 mm/hr. Further workup shows normal renal function and liver enzymes. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of difficulty walking and a noticeable 'slapping' gait. He reports that he occasionally trips over his toes and has developed progressive weakness in his right leg. On examination, there is weakness in dorsiflexion of the right foot and decreased sensation over the dorsum of the foot. Reflexes are normal. The patient denies any recent trauma. His medical history is significant for a previous diagnosis of type 1 diabetes mellitus. What is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic fatigue and weight gain over the last 6 months. He mentions experiencing joint pain in his hands and knees, particularly in the morning, though it tends to improve as the day progresses. He denies alcohol use and smokes occasionally. His past medical history is notable for hypertension. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 130/85 mmHg, heart rate is 76/min, and temperature is 98.6°F (37°C). The thyroid is diffusely enlarged and non-tender on palpation. Laboratory tests reveal elevated thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) and low free thyroxine (FT4) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 53-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, night sweats, and generalized fatigue. He reports experiencing some joint discomfort, mainly in his wrists and knees, but denies any significant swelling or morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable. Laboratory studies show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Blood cultures and initial infectious workups are negative. A chest X-ray is performed and shows normal findings. On further questioning, he mentions recent travel to a rural area for a family reunion. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 16-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling. He reports that over the past two weeks, he has experienced pain that seems to move from his knees to his elbows, then to his wrists. He also notes that he had a sore throat about three weeks ago which resolved without treatment. On physical examination, he has a low-grade fever and erythematous, swollen joints with tenderness upon palpation. His heart examination reveals a soft systolic murmur at the apex. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 22-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain that seems to move from one joint to another over the past two weeks. Initially, he experienced pain in his right wrist, which then resolved, but now he has pain in his left knee. He also mentions a sore throat a few weeks ago that he thought was a common cold. On physical examination, there is mild swelling and tenderness of the left knee with no evident deformity. He denies any recent trauma or significant past medical history. Laboratory workup reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old male presents to his rheumatologist with a 3-month history of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists. He describes morning stiffness lasting over an hour and notices that his symptoms improve slightly as the day progresses. His medical history is significant for seasonal allergies but is otherwise unremarkable. On physical examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive weakness in his shoulders and hips over the past few months. He states that he struggles to lift his arms to brush his hair and has difficulty climbing stairs. He denies experiencing any significant joint pain but mentions occasional fatigue and mild hand stiffness in the mornings. He has a history of well-controlled hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Physical examination reveals decreased muscle strength in the proximal muscles of the upper and lower limbs, while distal muscle strength is preserved. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of purplish, net-like discoloration on his legs and recurrent episodes of painful swelling in his veins. He describes a history of recurrent miscarriages and mild joint pain, which he attributed to his physical job as a carpenter. He reports significant fatigue, but denies any fever or weight loss. Physical examination reveals livedo reticularis on the lower extremities and palpable cords in his legs. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Anticardiolipin antibodies and lupus anticoagulant tests return positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint swelling and pain in both hands and wrists for the past 3 months. The symptoms are worse in the morning and improve as the day progresses. He reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fevers. He has a history of hypertension, controlled with medication, and smokes half a pack of cigarettes per day. He denies any recent infections or significant travel history.

On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 35 mm/hr and a C-reactive protein (CRP) level of 12 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor (RF) is positive, and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are markedly elevated. X-rays of the hands reveal periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing.

Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 56-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of difficulty walking and tripping over his left foot for the past month. He reports that the weakness has been progressive and denies any pain. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. His current medications include metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. On physical examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion of the left foot, decreased sensation over the dorsum of the foot and lateral calf, and a high-stepping gait. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ throughout. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with right shoulder pain that has been progressively worsening over the past 3 months. He describes the pain as a dull ache, exacerbated by movement, especially when reaching overhead or behind his back. He denies any trauma to the shoulder but mentions occasional stiffness in the morning lasting about 15 minutes. His medical history is significant for controlled hypertension. On examination, there is a noticeable restriction in the active and passive range of motion, particularly in abduction and external rotation. The shoulder joint appears normal with no swelling or erythema, and there is no tenderness to palpation. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a fever of 102.2°F (39°C) and generalized fatigue that has persisted for the past three weeks. He reports a 10-pound weight loss over this period and occasional joint pain, particularly in his knees and wrists. He describes his pain as mild and intermittent, without morning stiffness. He has a medical history significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. Vital signs reveal a blood pressure of 132/84 mmHg, pulse of 88/min, and respirations of 18/min. Physical examination shows no focal tenderness or swelling in the joints. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with normal complete blood count and liver function tests. Blood cultures are pending. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.F: Perform a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis
options.G: Refer to rheumatology for suspected rheumatoid arthritis

question: A 24-year-old male presents to the clinic with a two-year history of lower back pain and stiffness. He reports that the pain is worse in the morning and improves with activity as the day progresses. He also notes bouts of fatigue and occasional joint pain in his hips and shoulders. The patient denies any trauma or injury. His father has a history of psoriasis. Physical examination reveals reduced range of motion in the lumbar spine and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is most closely associated with this patient's condition?

A 31-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain following a fall from a bicycle earlier that day. He reports landing on his outstretched hand, and now experiences pain and swelling around the wrist. He denies any numbness or tingling in his fingers but is unable to move his wrist without significant pain. On examination, there is localized swelling and tenderness over the anatomical snuffbox. An X-ray of the wrist shows no obvious fracture. 

Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.A: Immobilization with a splint and follow-up X-rays in 10-14 days
options.F: Prescription of oral non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs)

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of progressive fatigue, joint pain, and bruising. He notes that he has been experiencing pain in his wrists and knees, and has been more tired than usual, despite adequate sleep. He denies any recent infections, bleeding, or gastrointestinal symptoms. His medical history is significant for well-controlled diabetes mellitus. On physical examination, the patient has multiple ecchymoses on his arms and legs, pallor, and mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory studies reveal pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 9.5 g/dL, platelets of 45,000/mm^3, and a white blood cell count of 2,500/mm^3. The peripheral blood smear shows teardrop-shaped red blood cells and a leukoerythroblastic picture. Bone marrow biopsy is performed, revealing hypocellular marrow with fibrosis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic with a three-week history of joint pain and a red, circular rash on his left thigh. He recalls being bitten by a tick while hiking a month ago. He describes the joint pain as migratory, affecting his knees, wrists, and elbows. He denies any recent fever, weight loss, or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. On physical examination, an erythematous rash with central clearing is noted on the left thigh. Joint examination reveals tenderness in multiple joints with mild swelling. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), but rheumatoid factor (RF) is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male visits his physician with concerns about progressive weakness, particularly noticeable when climbing stairs or lifting objects above his head. He reports this has been gradually worsening over the past 6 months. He denies any significant joint pain or morning stiffness, although he has experienced occasional fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and he is not on any medications. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatine kinase levels. Electromyography is performed and shows a myopathic pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past 8 months. He also reports experiencing swelling and discomfort around the jaw area, which his dentist suggested could be related to his salivary glands. He denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. His past medical history is unremarkable except for frequent dental cavities. On physical examination, there is noticeable enlargement of the parotid glands bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with a speckled pattern. Given this clinical presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of nasal congestion and recurrent sinus infections. He reports a persistent cough, hemoptysis, and unintentional weight loss over the past few weeks. His medical history is notable for hypertension, and he has no known drug allergies. Physical examination reveals crackles in both lung bases and mild edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and creatinine level. A chest radiograph shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. Urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 15-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to a rash and joint pain. The rash appeared 3 days ago and is mainly located on his lower legs and buttocks. He also reports abdominal pain and some episodes of blood in his urine. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), pulse is 85/min, respirations are 18/min, and blood pressure is 120/70 mm Hg. Physical examination shows palpable purpura on the lower extremities and mild swelling of the ankles. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. His complete blood count shows a normal white blood cell count, and his platelet count is within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old male presents to the emergency department complaining of severe pain in his right wrist following a fall onto an outstretched hand. He notes that he tripped on a sidewalk, landing on his hands to break the fall. On examination, there is notable swelling and tenderness over the distal radius, with some restriction of wrist movement due to pain. His medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. X-rays of the wrist reveal a fracture of the distal radius with dorsal angulation. Laboratory studies show normal calcium and phosphorus levels. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

options.A: Start glucocorticoids for suspected inflammatory arthritis
options.B: Order rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.D: Surgical fixation with a volar plate

question: A 45-year-old man visits his primary care physician with complaints of persistent joint pain and a rash. He reports that these symptoms developed approximately two weeks after a hiking trip in a wooded area, where he recalls removing a tick from his leg. The joint pain is particularly prominent in the knees and elbows, with associated swelling and morning stiffness lasting about 30 minutes. The rash is erythematous and has a target-like appearance, observed on his trunk and extremities. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he has no known allergies. On examination, he has a temperature of 100.4°F (38°C) with mild tenderness and swelling in the affected joints. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, and serologic testing is pending. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with unexplained weight gain and swelling around his eyes for the past month. He also reports morning joint stiffness lasting about an hour, predominantly affecting his wrists and knees. His medical history includes well-managed hyperlipidemia. He denies fever, changes in appetite, or any recent infections. On physical examination, he is found to have periorbital edema and mild pitting edema in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies reveal low serum albumin and elevated creatinine. Urinalysis shows proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 24-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a three-week history of diffuse joint pain, a facial rash that worsens with sun exposure, and new-onset mouth sores. He reports increasing fatigue but denies fever or recent infections. His medical history is significant for occasional episodes of joint stiffness, but he has never sought medical treatment for these symptoms. Physical examination reveals a maculopapular rash across the cheeks and nose, sparing the nasolabial folds, and shallow ulcers in the oral cavity. Basic laboratory tests show a mild anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He reports that these symptoms started shortly after recovering from a viral upper respiratory infection. He denies any joint swelling but mentions mild stiffness in the mornings that improves within an hour. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications regularly. He denies fever, rash, or weight loss. On examination, he appears tired but is in no acute distress. Musculoskeletal exam reveals tenderness over multiple muscle groups but no joint effusions or deformities. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, C-reactive protein at the upper limit of normal, and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling lasting for 4 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 90 minutes and difficulty gripping objects. He denies any recent infections or trauma. His medical history is significant for hypertension managed with lisinopril. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. X-rays of his hands show periarticular osteopenia. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for this patient's condition?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling sensations in his arms and fingers for the past three months. He reports the pain worsens with certain neck movements and sometimes experiences weakness in gripping objects. His medical history includes mild hypertension, managed with diet and exercise. Physical examination reveals decreased range of motion in the neck and diminished sensation in the C6 dermatome. Deep tendon reflexes are 2+ and symmetrical. A cervical spine MRI shows disc protrusion at C5-C6 causing mild foraminal stenosis. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 55-year-old male comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of left shoulder pain. He describes the pain as a dull ache that is present at rest and worsens with movement. The patient reports difficulty reaching overhead and putting on his jacket. He denies any recent trauma. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia. On examination, there is tenderness over the left shoulder and a significant reduction in both active and passive range of motion, especially in abduction and external rotation. An X-ray of the shoulder is unremarkable. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of fatigue, generalized weakness, and joint pain that has been persistent for the last three months. He mentions that the pain is more pronounced in his wrists and knees. Additionally, he has noticed increased bruising and bleeding gums. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications. Physical examination reveals pallor and petechiae on his arms and legs. Laboratory studies show pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,500/mm³, and platelet count of 70,000/mm³. An additional work-up reveals elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with persistent fatigue, generalized musculoskeletal pain, and difficulty sleeping. He reports the pain is widespread, affecting both upper and lower body, and often wakes him up at night. He describes it as a dull, aching pain that has been ongoing for several months. He also complains of feeling unrefreshed after sleep and struggles to maintain focus during the day. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. He denies alcohol or drug use, and his family history is non-contributory. On examination, there is tenderness to palpation at multiple tender points throughout his body. Laboratory tests, including rheumatoid factor and inflammatory markers, are within normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of progressive muscle weakness over the past few months. He reports difficulty climbing stairs and lifting objects above his head. The weakness is predominantly in his shoulders and hips. He denies any significant joint pain or swelling but mentions occasional mild fatigue. On physical examination, there is notable weakness of his proximal muscles, but no deformities or tenderness in the joints. His laboratory findings reveal elevated creatine kinase levels and mild elevation of ESR. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising for the past month. He reports that the pain is primarily in his wrists and knees, and is worse in the morning, lasting for about an hour. He also notes feeling unusually tired despite adequate sleep and has noticed several bruises on his arms and legs that he cannot attribute to any trauma. His medical history is unremarkable. On examination, he appears pale, with tenderness and mild swelling of the wrists and knees. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,000/mm3, and platelets of 90,000/mm3. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 54-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of increasing fatigue, joint pain, and easy bruising over the past three months. He reports pain and stiffness in his hands and knees that worsens in the morning but improves throughout the day. He also notes frequent nosebleeds and occasional bleeding gums. He denies any significant alcohol use and has no known allergies. His medical history is notable for treated hypertension. Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on the lower extremities, and tenderness in multiple joints. Laboratory tests show pancytopenia with hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,800/µL, and platelets of 85,000/µL. Further testing reveals elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

options.D: Systemic lupus erythematosus with lupus nephritis

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and stiffness in his fingers and wrists, as well as scaly red patches on his elbows and knees. He reports that the joint discomfort began a few months ago and has been progressively worsening. He denies any morning stiffness, but notes that the pain varies in intensity during the day. His skin lesions have been present for several years but have recently become more noticeable. On examination, there are swollen and tender distal interphalangeal joints of the fingers, and nail pitting is observed. There is no evidence of synovial thickening or symmetric involvement of the MCP or PIP joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and negative rheumatoid factor. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the emergency department with left wrist pain after slipping on ice and falling forward onto his outstretched hand. He reports immediate pain and swelling in his wrist. He denies any prior history of wrist injuries or joint disorders. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and visible swelling over the wrist. An x-ray of the wrist is ordered and shows a fracture of the distal radius. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 52-year-old male is evaluated for three months of fatigue and increasing joint pain in his wrists and knees. He also notes occasional fevers and night sweats. His past medical history is significant for a peptic ulcer, for which he takes omeprazole. He denies any recent infections but mentions weight loss over the past two months. Physical examination shows pallor and tenderness in multiple small joints of the hands and knees but no synovitis. Laboratory tests reveal hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, a leukocyte count of 3,500/mm^3, and a platelet count of 110,000/mm^3. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. Bone marrow biopsy shows hypocellular marrow with increased fat spaces. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male comes to the clinic with complaints of lower back pain that has been persistent for the past six months, particularly in the morning, and is associated with stiffness that improves throughout the day with activity. He also reports pain in his buttocks and occasional swelling in his left knee. He denies any history of trauma. His family history is significant for inflammatory bowel disease in his father. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the sacroiliac joints and mild restriction in lumbar spine mobility. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Based on these findings, which of the following is the next best step in management?

options.E: Plain X-ray of the sacroiliac joints

question: A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and intermittent low-grade fevers. He also reports joint pain in his hands and wrists, which are stiff in the morning for about 30 minutes. On examination, he has several firm, non-tender subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces of his elbows and forearms. He denies any skin rash, weight loss, or gastrointestinal symptoms. His medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled with medication. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and normal anti-citrullinated protein antibody (ACPA) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling for the past 3 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 90 minutes, which improves as the day progresses. He has a history of hypertension, controlled with lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. Serologic tests show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. Joint radiographs demonstrate juxta-articular osteopenia and soft tissue swelling. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. He also reports generalized swelling in multiple areas including his neck, armpits, and groin. He denies any recent infections, fever, or significant joint pain. He has a 20-year history of smoking but quit 5 years ago. His temperature is 37.2°C (99°F), blood pressure is 130/82 mmHg, pulse is 88/min, and respirations are 18/min. Physical examination reveals symmetric, non-tender lymphadenopathy in the cervical, axillary, and inguinal regions. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and normal liver and renal function tests. Imaging reveals multiple enlarged lymph nodes without any organomegaly. A lymph node biopsy shows effacement of the normal nodal architecture with the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 29-year-old male presents to the clinic with joint pain, a rash, and oral ulcers. He reports that the rash worsens when he spends time in the sun and has been present for about 3 weeks. The joint pain primarily affects his wrists and knees and is accompanied by morning stiffness lasting around 20 minutes. He also reports fatigue and intermittent, non-bloody diarrhea. On physical examination, there is an erythematous rash across the bridge of his nose and cheeks, and multiple shallow ulcers are noted on the buccal mucosa. Laboratory tests reveal leukopenia and a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with concerns about a skin rash and joint pain. He has noticed red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees and reports joint swelling and pain, particularly in his fingers and toes. There is no history of similar symptoms in the past. He has a family history of psoriasis. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 128/76 mmHg, pulse is 75/min, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces, and dactylitis is noted in several digits. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the dermatology clinic with a rash on his face and joint pain. He notes that the rash appears worse after spending time in the sun. Over the past few weeks, he has also experienced fatigue and stiffness in his wrists and knees, particularly in the mornings. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. On physical examination, a well-demarcated erythematous rash is noted over his cheeks and nasal bridge, sparing the nasolabial folds. Laboratory tests reveal anemia and mild leukopenia. His temperature is 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg, pulse is 80/min, respirations are 16/min, and oxygen saturation is 98% on room air. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 10-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents because of a rash and blood in his urine. They report that the rash started on his lower legs two days ago and resembles small red or purple dots. Moreover, he had a sore throat about a week ago, which resolved without any treatment. On further questioning, the parents mention that he complained of abdominal pain and joint pain in his knees and ankles during the past few days. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the buttocks and lower extremities. Laboratory studies show hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 62-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of persistent bone pain for the past few months. He has also noticed increased fatigue and occasional nausea. He denies any recent trauma or changes in activity levels. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both well-controlled with medication. He takes no other medications and does not use tobacco or alcohol. Laboratory tests reveal a serum calcium level of 11.5 mg/dL (normal: 8.5-10.2 mg/dL), an intact parathyroid hormone (PTH) level of 95 pg/mL (normal: 15-65 pg/mL), and elevated alkaline phosphatase. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of lower back pain that has been progressively worsening over the past year. The pain is dull and worse in the mornings, taking several hours to improve. He reports that the pain improves with activity but worsens with rest. He denies any significant trauma or recent injuries. He also experiences occasional fatigue and stiffness in his hips. His family history is significant for an uncle with a similar condition. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion in the lumbar spine. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP), with negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the dermatology clinic with complaints of a persistent skin rash and joint pain. He reports that the rash has been present for several months and recently started noticing swelling and pain in his fingers and toes, particularly on the left side. The patient denies any significant medical history and does not take any medications regularly. He reports that his father had similar skin and joint issues, but he is unsure of the diagnosis. On physical examination, there are erythematous plaques with silvery scaling over the extensor surfaces of the elbows and knees, as well as dactylitis of the left index and middle fingers. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated C-reactive protein, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 45-year-old male presents to the clinic with sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling, which began after playing a game of tennis three days ago. He describes the pain as sharp and notes significant difficulty in bearing weight on the affected leg. The patient has no history of previous knee injuries or chronic joint issues. His medical history is unremarkable except for hyperlipidemia managed with atorvastatin. On examination, the right knee is swollen and warm to touch, with limited range of motion due to pain. There is no erythema, and the contralateral knee is normal. Laboratory results show elevated inflammatory markers. An X-ray of the knee reveals no fracture or dislocation. Aspiration of the joint shows cloudy fluid with elevated white blood cell count and neutrophils. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to the clinic with a history of recurrent fevers and night sweats over the past two months. He reports feeling fatigued and has noticed swelling in his neck and armpits. The patient denies weight loss or rash but mentions occasional joint pain in his knees and wrists. He reports no significant medical history and has not traveled recently. On physical examination, there is palpable lymphadenopathy in the cervical and axillary regions. Blood tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. A lymph node biopsy is performed. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic because of a 3-month history of morning stiffness and swelling in his hands and wrists, which improves as the day goes on. He reports that the joints on both sides of his body are affected. His past medical history is unremarkable. He takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. His vital signs are within normal limits. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). The rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. An X-ray of his hands shows periarticular osteopenia and joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 40-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion, recurring sinus infections, and a cough producing blood-tinged sputum. He also reports recent onset of malaise and joint pain in his knees and elbows. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he is a non-smoker. A chest radiograph reveals multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules. Laboratory studies show elevated serum creatinine, hematuria, and red cell casts on urinalysis. ANCA serology is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man with a history of type 2 diabetes and dyslipidemia visits his primary care physician due to recent onset headaches and fatigue. He mentions that his blood pressure, previously well-controlled with lifestyle changes, has been consistently elevated during recent home monitoring. On physical examination, his blood pressure is 165/95 mmHg. Laboratory studies reveal an elevated serum creatinine of 1.8 mg/dL (increased from a baseline of 1.0 mg/dL six months ago) and a urinalysis shows mild proteinuria. An abdominal ultrasound demonstrates asymmetrical kidney sizes. The patient denies any significant joint pain but reports occasional morning stiffness. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in managing this patient's condition?

A 38-year-old male comes to the clinic complaining of diffuse body aches and fatigue over the last two weeks. He reports that these symptoms began after recovering from a flu-like illness. He denies any recent travel, trauma, or exposure to sick contacts beyond his immediate family. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. Physical examination reveals tenderness in multiple muscle groups, particularly in the shoulders and thighs, but joint examination is normal. Laboratory results show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 56-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a severe headache and elevated blood pressure. He reports that he has been experiencing headaches and fatigue over the past few weeks. His medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Physical examination reveals a blood pressure of 180/110 mm Hg and bilateral lower extremity edema. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine. Urinalysis demonstrates proteinuria and microscopic hematuria. He also notes joint stiffness in the morning that lasts for about an hour, which he attributes to aging. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's current symptoms?

A 60-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to fatigue and joint pain. He describes the pain as a symmetric aching sensation affecting both wrists and several finger joints over the past 6 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting over an hour, which improves gradually throughout the day. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional episodes of heartburn. Physical examination reveals synovial thickening and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and wrist joints bilaterally. Laboratory tests show normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A rheumatoid factor test returns positive. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 58-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with bilateral hip pain that has been progressively worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a dull ache that is worse with activity and alleviated by rest. He denies any morning stiffness lasting more than 15 minutes. He has a history of type 2 diabetes mellitus and hyperlipidemia, for which he takes metformin and atorvastatin. On examination, there is tenderness over the groin area and decreased range of motion in both hips. There is no redness or swelling noted. Laboratory results reveal a normal erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). An x-ray of the pelvis shows joint space narrowing and osteophyte formation in the hip joints. What is the most likely diagnosis?

options.C: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head

question: A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic because of chronic lower back pain for the past 8 months. He describes the pain as dull and constant, with significant morning stiffness that lasts for over an hour. The pain improves throughout the day with physical activity. He also reports occasional pain in his knees and fatigue. He works as a software engineer and does not recall any recent trauma or injuries. His medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of increased fatigue and weight gain over the past several months. He mentions feeling cold more often and notes that his skin has become dry. He also reports occasional joint pain, particularly in his hands, but denies any significant swelling or morning stiffness lasting longer than 15 minutes. His past medical history is significant for hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. On examination, his temperature is 97.5°F (36.4°C), pulse is 58/min, blood pressure is 135/85 mmHg, and respirations are 14/min. Physical examination reveals mild periorbital puffiness and dry, coarse skin. Laboratory tests show a thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) level of 8.5 μIU/mL (normal: 0.4-4.0 μIU/mL), with a free T4 level of 0.7 ng/dL (normal: 0.8-2.0 ng/dL). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 72-year-old man presents to the emergency department with severe back pain that began suddenly while he was attempting to lift a heavy box. He describes the pain as sharp and located in the mid-thoracic region. He has a history of osteoporosis and has been taking calcium and vitamin D supplements, but no other medications. He denies any recent trauma or falls. On examination, he has tenderness over the thoracic spine and limited range of motion due to pain. Neurological examination is unremarkable. His vital signs are normal. A plain X-ray of the thoracic spine is shown in Figure A, demonstrating loss of vertebral height. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of persistent nasal congestion and recurrent episodes of sinusitis. He has been experiencing fatigue, joint pain, and a cough productive of yellow sputum. Over the past two months, he has also noticed blood in his urine. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he denies any recent travel or exposure to known respiratory irritants. Physical examination reveals tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and crackles heard on lung auscultation. Laboratory tests show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and creatinine level of 2.1 mg/dL. Urinalysis reveals red cell casts. Chest X-ray demonstrates bilateral patchy infiltrates. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while playing soccer. He reports immediate pain and swelling in the wrist area, which has worsened over the last few hours. He has no significant past medical history and does not take any medications. On examination, there is tenderness, swelling, and limited range of motion in the right wrist. An X-ray of the wrist is pending. His vital signs are stable. What is the most appropriate initial management for this patient's condition?

A 16-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and swelling that seems to migrate from one joint to another. He reports having a sore throat two weeks ago, which subsided without antibiotic treatment. Upon examination, he has fever and tenderness in his wrists and ankles. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, and an ECG shows prolonged PR interval. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of a rash on his face and joint pain in his hands and knees. He notes that the rash is particularly sensitive to sunlight and has developed over the bridge of his nose and cheeks. Additionally, he reports experiencing ulcers in his mouth that have been persistent over the past few weeks. His medical history is notable for occasional headaches but is otherwise unremarkable. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and leukopenia. An antinuclear antibody (ANA) test returns positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 54-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with increasing joint pain and stiffness over the past several months. He reports that the stiffness is particularly severe in the morning, lasting for more than an hour before it eases. His medical history is unremarkable apart from occasional headaches. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally, with noticeable ulnar deviation of the fingers. Laboratory studies show positive rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. X-rays of the hands reveal joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and unintentional weight loss over the past three months. He reports decreased appetite and occasional night sweats. He has a history of high blood pressure, which is controlled with medication. On examination, he appears pale. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin level of 9.5 g/dL, hematocrit of 28%, and mean corpuscular volume of 78 fL. Serum iron studies show low serum iron, low ferritin, and a high total iron-binding capacity. He has mild tenderness in the right upper quadrant, but no hepatosplenomegaly is noted. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and skin changes that have developed over the past year. He notes that his joints, primarily in the fingers and toes, are swollen and sometimes painful, but the discomfort is not consistent in location. He also shows skin lesions that are red, scaly, and located primarily on his elbows and knees. He denies significant morning stiffness but mentions fatigue. His past medical history is unremarkable except for occasional mild ailments. Laboratory tests reveal mild elevation of inflammatory markers, and an x-ray of his hands shows soft tissue swelling without erosions. A family history reveals that his mother had a similar skin condition. Considering his symptoms and family history, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 6-month history of joint pain and stiffness. He describes the pain as affecting his wrists, fingers, and knees bilaterally, with morning stiffness lasting over an hour. He also reports fatigue and occasional low-grade fever. His family history is significant for rheumatoid arthritis in his mother. On physical examination, there is swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. X-rays of the hands show joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old male is brought to the clinic by his parents due to joint pains and fatigue. The patient reports experiencing a sore throat two weeks ago that resolved without treatment. Since then, he has developed pain and swelling in his knees, ankles, and wrists, with the symptoms migrating between joints. He has no significant past medical history and denies recent trauma. Physical examination shows erythema and swelling in the affected joints, and a faint, pink rash is noted on his trunk. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A rapid streptococcal antigen test is positive. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of persistent lymphadenopathy and unintentional weight loss. He reports feeling fatigued and experiencing night sweats. On physical examination, there is palpable cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy. Laboratory tests show anemia and elevated lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) levels. He denies any joint pain or morning stiffness, and his past medical history is unremarkable. A lymph node biopsy is performed, revealing a proliferation of atypical lymphocytes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old male presents to the emergency department with sudden severe back pain after lifting a heavy box. He describes it as a sharp pain localized to the mid-back area. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or radiating pain. The patient has a history of osteoporosis and chronic corticosteroid use due to polymyalgia rheumatica. He lives independently and is usually active, but notes increased fatigue over the past few months. On examination, there is tenderness over the thoracic spine, but no neurological deficits are noted. Laboratory investigations reveal normal inflammatory markers and renal function. An X-ray of the spine is performed and shown in Figure A. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue for the past two weeks. He mentions that he had a viral infection with fever and sore throat about three weeks ago, which has resolved. However, since then, he has been experiencing generalized muscle pain and lethargy. He denies any joint swelling or morning stiffness. On examination, there is no synovitis observed, and his vital signs are within normal limits. Laboratory tests reveal normal inflammatory markers and negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of joint pain and swelling in both hands and wrists for the past two months. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour and feeling fatigued. He denies any recent trauma or fever. His medical history is significant for hypertension, well-controlled on medication. Physical examination reveals tender, swollen, and warm joints in bilateral wrists and metacarpophalangeal joints. Blood tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies are positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male arrives at the emergency department with pain in his right wrist following a fall while playing basketball. He reports landing directly on his outstretched hand. He experiences significant pain when trying to move his wrist and notes some swelling. There is no numbness or tingling in his fingers. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On physical examination, tenderness is noted over the anatomical snuffbox. There is no visible deformity, but mild swelling is observed. Passive range of motion is limited by pain, especially during ulnar deviation. An X-ray of the wrist is obtained. Based on the clinical presentation and examination findings, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of worsening fatigue, joint pain, and recurrent nosebleeds. He reports feeling unusually tired even after a full night's sleep and has been experiencing pain in his wrists and knees. He also mentions easy bruising and bleeding gums. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional upper respiratory infections. Physical examination reveals pallor, petechiae on the extremities, and mild tenderness in multiple joints. Laboratory studies show hemoglobin of 9.0 g/dL, white blood cell count of 2,800 /µL, and platelets of 90,000 /µL. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

A 55-year-old man presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of persistent neck pain and intermittent numbness in his right arm that he has experienced for the past six months. The symptoms are aggravated by neck movements. He reports no recent trauma but has a history of working as a manual laborer for over 30 years. On physical examination, there is limited range of motion in the cervical spine and decreased sensation in the C6 dermatome. Reflexes are normal, and no muscle weakness is noted in the upper extremities. An MRI of the cervical spine is performed and reveals disc degeneration with foraminal narrowing at the C5-C6 level. Which of the following is the most appropriate management for this patient's condition?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of fatigue and a 5-kg (11-lb) unintentional weight loss. He also notes intermittent joint pain involving his wrists and knees. His medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or drink alcohol. He works as a software engineer and reports no recent travel or changes in his lifestyle. On physical examination, he appears pale, and there is mild tenderness over the wrists. Laboratory studies reveal hemoglobin of 9.8 g/dL, leukocyte count of 6000/mm3, platelet count of 210,000/mm3, and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Serum iron studies show low serum iron, low ferritin, and high total iron-binding capacity (TIBC). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain, fatigue, and recurrent episodes of easy bruising over the past 3 months. He reports feeling increasingly tired and has noticed some swelling and stiffness in his wrists and knees, particularly in the morning. He denies any significant weight loss but mentions occasional night sweats. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or consume alcohol. Physical examination reveals pale conjunctivae and mild splenomegaly. Laboratory tests show hemoglobin of 9.2 g/dL, white blood cell count of 3,200/mm³, and platelets of 80,000/mm³. A peripheral blood smear is notable for pancytopenia. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man comes to the clinic because of generalized body aches and fatigue that started after recovering from a viral upper respiratory infection two weeks ago. He describes the pain as diffuse and affecting his muscles more than his joints. He denies any swelling or redness in the joints. The fatigue has been persistent, and he reports difficulty concentrating at work. Physical examination is unremarkable except for mild tenderness over several muscle groups without swelling or erythema. Laboratory tests reveal a normal complete blood count and elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic due to a 2-month history of progressive difficulty walking. He describes weakness in his legs, which has worsened over time, making it challenging to climb stairs and stand for long periods. He denies any numbness or tingling but has noticed occasional muscle cramps. He has a history of well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus. On examination, there is evident weakness in the hip flexors and knee extensors, with normal sensation. Reflexes are diminished in the lower extremities. Laboratory studies show an elevated creatinine kinase level. An electromyography is ordered and shows myopathic changes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 12-year-old boy is brought to the clinic by his parents due to fatigue and a rash on his legs. They first noticed the rash a week ago, which began as small spots that have coalesced into larger patches on his lower extremities. He also reports dark-colored urine and mild abdominal pain. On examination, there are palpable purpura on his buttocks and legs. His blood pressure is 110/70 mmHg. Laboratory tests reveal hematuria and mild proteinuria. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of widespread muscle pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping over the past 6 months. He states that the pain is persistent, affecting his shoulders, back, and thighs, and is not relieved by rest. He also notes feeling tired throughout the day despite sleeping for 8-9 hours at night. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there are tender points at several muscle junctions, particularly in his neck, shoulders, and hips. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and C-reactive protein level. Thyroid function tests are also within normal limits. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man comes to the clinic because of painful red nodules on his shins and joint pain over the past two weeks. He reports that he first noticed the nodules after recovering from a sore throat. He has been experiencing similar joint pains in his wrists and ankles, making it difficult to work as a carpenter. He denies fever, weight loss, or recent travel. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily but does not use alcohol or illicit drugs. Physical examination shows multiple tender, erythematous nodules on both shins. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titer is elevated. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with a 9-month history of progressive difficulty walking. He reports that his legs feel weak and often give out, especially after walking short distances. He denies numbness, tingling, or back pain but mentions occasional muscle cramps in his legs. He has no significant past medical history and takes no medications. He works as an office manager and is a former smoker, having quit 10 years ago. On examination, there is proximal muscle weakness in his lower extremities, but no sensory deficits. Reflexes are diminished in the lower limbs. Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatine kinase (CK) levels and negative rheumatoid factor. An electromyography (EMG) study is performed, showing a myopathic pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of dry eyes and dry mouth for the past six months. He reports frequent use of artificial tears and describes a constant need to sip water throughout the day. Additionally, he notices a sense of fullness in his cheeks and has experienced mild swelling in this area. He also mentions occasional joint pain and generalized fatigue. He denies any fever, weight loss, or skin rashes. On physical examination, there is bilateral enlargement of the parotid glands. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count, negative rheumatoid factor, and elevated antinuclear antibodies. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 72-year-old man visits the emergency department with acute onset of severe lower back pain after lifting a heavy object at home. He describes the pain as sharp and it radiates anteriorly into the abdomen. He denies any recent trauma or history of malignancy. He also notes some mild fatigue over the past few months. His past medical history includes long-standing smoking and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. On examination, there is tenderness over the lumbar vertebrae but no neurological deficits. A lateral X-ray of the lumbar spine shows a wedge-shaped vertebra. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic due to difficulty walking and frequent tripping for the past month. He also reports numbness and tingling along the outer side of his right leg and top of his foot. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus and mild hypertension. Physical examination reveals weakness in dorsiflexion and eversion of the right foot, with diminished sensation in the distribution of the right common peroneal nerve. Reflexes are normal. The patient denies any trauma or recent injuries. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 4-month history of neck pain and intermittent tingling in both of his arms. He reports the tingling is most noticeable when he wakes up in the morning and sometimes occurs when he tilts his head backward while working at his desk. The neck pain is described as a dull ache and is exacerbated by movement. He denies any recent trauma, fever, or significant weight change. His medical history includes well-controlled hypertension. On physical examination, there is a reduced range of motion in the cervical spine. Spurling's maneuver reproduces the paresthesias in his arms. Neurological examination reveals diminished biceps and brachioradialis reflexes bilaterally. MRI of the cervical spine shows degenerative changes and disk osteophyte complexes at C5-C6 and C6-C7. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with a facial rash that has worsened over the past two weeks. He reports the rash is particularly pronounced after being in the sun. Additionally, he has been experiencing joint pain in his hands and knees, along with fatigue. He denies any fevers, weight loss, or morning stiffness lasting more than 30 minutes. On examination, there is a well-demarcated erythematous rash across his cheeks and the bridge of his nose. Laboratory tests reveal a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) test. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain in the ankles and knees. These symptoms began two weeks ago following a sore throat that resolved without treatment. He reports feeling generally fatigued and has had intermittent fevers, but denies any weight loss or night sweats. His past medical history is unremarkable. He works as a school teacher and has not traveled recently. Physical examination reveals tender erythematous nodules on both shins and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with chronic lower back pain and stiffness that has been worsening over the past few months. He notices that the stiffness is particularly pronounced in the morning and lasts for over an hour, but seems to improve somewhat with activity. He denies any recent trauma or significant weight change. His family history is notable for an uncle with psoriasis. He is a non-smoker and drinks alcohol occasionally. On physical examination, the patient has reduced spinal mobility, with a positive Schober's test. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and knees, along with generalized fatigue and unintentional weight loss of 10 pounds. He denies any joint swelling or significant morning stiffness but reports occasional joint aches. His past medical history is notable for hypertension, which is well controlled with medication. On physical examination, firm, non-tender nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of both elbows and knees. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male comes to the clinic due to persistent neck pain and tingling sensations radiating down his right arm for the past six months. He denies any history of trauma or previous neck injuries. The pain is described as a dull ache that becomes sharp with certain movements, and the tingling occasionally extends into the fingers. He works as a software engineer and spends long hours at the computer. On physical examination, there is decreased range of motion of the cervical spine, and Spurling's test is positive. An EMG study reveals nerve conduction delay in the right C6 nerve root. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic complaining of fatigue and joint pain. He reports that over the last several months, he has experienced stiffness and swelling in his fingers, wrists, and knees, which is worse in the mornings and improves throughout the day. His past medical history is unremarkable. Physical examination reveals symmetric swelling and tenderness of the metacarpophalangeal joints and wrists. Laboratory studies show a hemoglobin level of 11.5 g/dL, with a mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of 90 fL, and an erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) of 55 mm/hr. A hand x-ray shows periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of fatigue and swelling in his legs. Over the past few months, he has noted a gradual increase in blood pressure readings at home. He denies any chest pain or shortness of breath. His past medical history includes benign prostatic hyperplasia and intermittent knee pain. On examination, his blood pressure is 160/100 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and there is periorbital edema. Laboratory tests reveal elevated creatinine levels and proteinuria. An ultrasound of the kidneys shows asymmetrical kidney sizes. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 32-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 2-week history of joint pain and a rash. He mentions that he recently returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States and recalls removing a tick from his leg. The joint pain is migratory and affects his knees and shoulders. On examination, there is an erythematous rash with central clearing on his back. Laboratory tests show mildly elevated ESR and normal CRP levels. He denies any recent gastrointestinal symptoms or travel outside the country. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 3-week history of intermittent fevers, night sweats, and joint pain. He reports feeling extremely fatigued and has noticed some weight loss. His past medical history is unremarkable, except for occasional knee pain which he attributes to an old sports injury. Laboratory tests reveal elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Physical examination is significant for mild synovitis of the wrists and ankles. Blood cultures and a chest X-ray are unremarkable. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step to further evaluate the cause of the patient's fever?

A 60-year-old man presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty walking due to weakness in his legs over the past six months.
