He reports occasional tingling sensations in his feet and notes that climbing stairs has become increasingly challenging. He denies any significant joint pain or swelling. His medical history is notable for well-controlled type 2 diabetes mellitus and hypertension. On physical examination, there is symmetrical weakness in the lower extremities, more pronounced in the proximal muscles. Sensory examination reveals decreased vibration sense in the feet. Reflexes are diminished at the knees and ankles. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and basic metabolic panel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain that has been ongoing for several months. He describes the pain as diffuse, with no specific joint involvement, and mentions that it is associated with significant fatigue and non-restorative sleep. He states that he wakes up feeling unrefreshed despite what he believes to be adequate hours of sleep. He denies any swelling or redness in the joints. His medical history is significant for mild hypertension, for which he takes lisinopril. He works as an accountant and reports that work-related stress has increased recently. On physical examination, there are tender points noted at various locations, but no synovitis is observed. Laboratory tests show normal inflammatory markers, including C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic complaining of diffuse body aches and fatigue that have persisted for the past two weeks. He mentions recovering from a viral illness recently, characterized by fever and a mild sore throat. He denies joint swelling, significant weight loss, or night sweats. He has a medical history of allergic rhinitis and no family history of autoimmune diseases. On examination, he has generalized tenderness to palpation over his muscles but no visible inflammation of the joints. Laboratory studies show elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate but normal rheumatoid factor and anti-nuclear antibody levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent nasal congestion, sinus pressure, and a chronic cough producing blood-tinged sputum. These symptoms have been progressively worsening over the past few months. He also reports fatigue and unintentional weight loss. His medical history is significant for recurrent sinus infections. On examination, he has tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and inspiratory crackles heard over the lung fields. Laboratory tests reveal elevated serum creatinine and hematuria. The C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate are elevated. A chest X-ray shows bilateral cavitary lesions in the upper lobes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old male presents to the emergency department with a 2-week history of non-blanching purpura on his lower extremities and hematuria. He reports mild joint pain and swelling in his ankles and knees, as well as occasional abdominal pain. He denies any recent infections, new medications, or changes in diet. Physical examination reveals palpable purpura on the lower legs and mild swelling of the knees. Laboratory tests show elevated serum creatinine and microscopic hematuria with red blood cell casts on urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent painful oral ulcers and genital sores. He also reports occasional joint pain in his knees and ankles, which he describes as aching and swollen, particularly in the mornings. He denies any significant weight loss, fever, or recent travel. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he does not take any medications regularly. Physical examination reveals multiple aphthous ulcers on the buccal mucosa and erythematous papules on the genital region. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count and inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man visits his physician with a 6-week history of foot drop and difficulty walking. He reports tripping over his toes and has noticed a slapping sound with each step. His medical history includes well-controlled diabetes mellitus. The patient denies pain, numbness, or tingling in his legs. On examination, there is weakness of dorsiflexion in the left foot. Sensory examination is normal, but there is atrophy of the anterior compartment of the leg. Deep tendon reflexes are intact. Electromyography and nerve conduction studies are performed. Which of the following is the most likely cause of his symptoms?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of painful, red nodules on his shins and joint pain. He reports that the joint pain started in his ankles and knees and later spread to his wrists. He denies any recent trauma or insect bites. His medical history is unremarkable except for occasional bouts of diarrhea. The patient does not smoke or drink alcohol. He works as a teacher and has had no recent travel. On physical examination, tender erythematous nodules are observed on the anterior aspect of his lower legs. Laboratory tests show elevated inflammatory markers, but no evidence of joint effusion or deformity. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 58-year-old man visits his primary care physician due to dry eyes and dry mouth that have been progressively worsening over the past year. He also reports experiencing recurrent swelling of his parotid glands. He denies any significant joint pain or swelling, but mentions occasional fatigue and mild discomfort in the hands. His medical history is significant for hypertension, which is well-managed with medication. He does not smoke or drink alcohol. On examination, bilateral parotid gland enlargement is noted. Schirmer test is positive, indicating reduced tear production. Laboratory tests reveal positive antinuclear antibodies (ANA) and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 45-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of acute onset of joint pain affecting both knees and ankles. He reports having returned from a hiking trip in South America two weeks ago. He denies any previous joint issues and states that he feels generally fatigued and has had occasional fevers. Physical examination reveals mild swelling and tenderness around the affected joints, but no redness or warmth is noted. Laboratory studies show elevated inflammatory markers. Travel history and clinical presentation raise suspicion for an infectious etiology. What is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's condition?

A 40-year-old male presents to the outpatient clinic with complaints of sudden onset of right knee pain and swelling following a basketball game two days ago. He describes the pain as sharp and rates it 8/10 in severity. He recalls a twisting injury but did not hear a pop. The knee has since become swollen, and he has difficulty bearing weight. He denies any previous joint issues, and his medical history is unremarkable. He is an avid weekend athlete and does not smoke or consume alcohol regularly. On examination, the right knee is swollen, with tenderness along the joint line and limited range of motion due to pain and effusion. There is no evidence of erythema or warmth. An x-ray shows no fractures. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of diffuse body aches and fatigue that started a week ago following a viral illness. He reports having a sore throat and mild fever last week that resolved on its own. Since then, he has been experiencing widespread muscular pain and a general feeling of fatigue. He denies any joint swelling, redness, or significant morning stiffness. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On physical examination, he has diffuse tenderness over his muscles without any signs of joint inflammation. Laboratory tests show normal complete blood count, normal electrolytes, and an elevated C-reactive protein level. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash that appeared after he returned from a hiking trip in the northeastern United States last month. He describes the pain as migratory, affecting his knees and elbows, and notes that it is more pronounced in the morning. He also reports feeling more fatigued than usual. On examination, there is an erythematous rash with a central clearing on his left thigh. Laboratory tests show mild leukocytosis and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of numbness and tingling in his hands and feet, which have progressed over the past six months. He also notes occasional burning pain in these areas, which is worse at night. His past medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago. He denies any recent changes in medication, and his blood sugar levels have been generally well-controlled. On physical examination, the patient has decreased sensation to light touch and pinprick in a stocking-glove distribution. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles bilaterally. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 16-year-old boy presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of red-purple spots on his legs and intermittent abdominal pain. He also reports blood in his urine over the past few days. He has no significant medical history and takes no medications. On examination, there is palpable purpura on the lower extremities. His blood pressure is 115/75 mm Hg, pulse is 82/min, and temperature is 36.8°C (98.2°F). Laboratory studies reveal elevated creatinine and hematuria with red blood cell casts on urinalysis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of persistent nasal congestion, episodes of nosebleeds, and a chronic cough that has developed over the past few months. He reports feeling fatigued and has experienced unintentional weight loss. On further questioning, he mentions occasional joint pain and swelling. Laboratory studies reveal elevated inflammatory markers, mild anemia, and renal function tests show elevated creatinine and blood urea nitrogen levels. A chest x-ray shows bilateral cavitary lesions, and urinalysis reveals hematuria and red blood cell casts. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man visits his primary care physician for evaluation of a red rash on his face that worsens with sun exposure. He reports that the rash first appeared a few months ago and is accompanied by joint pain, particularly in his wrists and knees. The joint pain is often worse in the morning and improves throughout the day. He also admits to feeling more fatigued than usual and has experienced intermittent fevers. He has no significant past medical history and does not smoke or drink alcohol. Family history is notable for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis. On examination, a well-demarcated malar rash is noted on the patient's face. Laboratory tests reveal mild anemia and elevated inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old man presents to the clinic with bilateral hip pain that has been worsening over the past six months. He describes the pain as a deep, aching discomfort that is worse after prolonged sitting and improves with movement. He denies any fever, weight loss, or recent trauma. He works as a software engineer and spends long hours sitting at a desk. Medical history is significant for previous ankle sprains but is otherwise unremarkable. Family history is significant for osteoarthritis in his father. On examination, the patient has a normal gait and full range of motion in both hips, although internal rotation is slightly painful. X-rays of the hips show mild joint space narrowing. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 26-year-old male comes to the clinic with a 1-month history of painful, red nodules on the anterior aspects of his lower legs and joint pain affecting his ankles and knees. He describes the joint pain as intermittent and migratory. He denies any recent fever, weight loss, or night sweats. He works as a schoolteacher and has not traveled recently. His medical history is significant for a recent upper respiratory tract infection 4 weeks ago. He is a non-smoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and denies recreational drug use. Physical examination reveals multiple erythematous, tender nodules on the shins with swelling and mild tenderness of the knee and ankle joints. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). A chest x-ray is performed and shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 33-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with a history of chronic lower back pain that has progressively worsened over the past two years. He describes the pain as dull and persistent, particularly noticeable in the morning or after periods of inactivity, and it improves with exercise. He reports stiffness in the lower back and hips. Over the past few months, he has also experienced occasional knee pain and fatigue. He denies any recent trauma or weight loss. His family history is significant for his father having similar back issues. Physical examination reveals limited spinal mobility, especially in the lumbar region. Laboratory tests show elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 35-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of chronic lower back pain that has persisted for more than six months. He describes the pain as dull and aching, primarily affecting the lower back and buttocks, and notes that it improves with exercise but worsens with rest. The patient reports morning stiffness lasting approximately 30 minutes. He has no significant past medical history and takes ibuprofen occasionally for pain relief. The patient denies any recent trauma. He is employed as a software engineer and has a sedentary lifestyle. Physical examination reveals decreased lumbar spine flexion and tenderness over the sacroiliac joints. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein levels. Radiographs of the spine reveal the presence of sacroiliitis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient?

A 52-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of unexplained weight gain, periorbital edema, and joint stiffness over the past few months. He mentions that his clothes have been feeling tighter, especially around his waist, and he has noticed puffiness around his eyes. His past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia, both of which are well-controlled with medication. He denies any recent changes in diet or exercise habits. The patient reports feeling more fatigued than usual and has experienced occasional joint pain, particularly in the mornings, but the stiffness subsides as the day progresses. Vital signs include a temperature of 98.6°F (37°C), blood pressure of 145/90 mmHg, pulse of 75/min, respirations of 16/min, and oxygen saturation of 98% on room air. On physical exam, periorbital puffiness and mild joint tenderness are noted. Laboratory tests show normal renal function and a mild elevation in inflammatory markers. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 23-year-old man visits the dermatology clinic due to a rash on his face that has worsened over the past two weeks. He notes that the rash seems to become more prominent after spending time outdoors. Additionally, he reports joint pain primarily in his wrists and knees, and he has noticed some sores in his mouth that are not painful. He denies any significant medical history and is not taking any medications. His vital signs are within normal limits. On examination, the rash is characterized by erythematous plaques with scaling distributed over the malar region. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) titer. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with complaints of persistent pain and swelling in his hands and wrists, which he has noticed over the last 8 months. The pain is symmetrical and is worse in the morning, lasting about 2 hours before improving. On examination, he has warm, swollen, and tender metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints bilaterally. Several subcutaneous nodules are palpated on his extensor surfaces. Laboratory studies reveal a positive rheumatoid factor and elevated anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 38-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of a mottled purplish discoloration on his legs that becomes more pronounced in cold weather. He also reports a history of recurrent deep vein thrombosis and pulmonary embolism for which he is on long-term anticoagulation therapy. Laboratory workup reveals normal CBC and renal function tests. His family history is significant for an uncle with systemic lupus erythematosus. On examination, his skin shows livedo reticularis. Which of the following conditions is most likely responsible for his symptoms?

A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with complaints of a purplish, net-like rash on his lower extremities and episodes of leg swelling and pain over the past few months. He reports the swelling is often accompanied by pain and redness, especially after prolonged periods of immobility. His past medical history is notable for recurrent unexplained blood clots for which he has been taking anticoagulants. He denies any joint pain or morning stiffness. Laboratory workup reveals a normal complete blood count and metabolic panel, but an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and positive lupus anticoagulant. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old man comes to the clinic with a 3-month history of tingling and numbness in his hands and feet. He describes a 'pins and needles' sensation that is more pronounced in the evenings. His medical history includes type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago, and hypertension. He is on metformin and lisinopril. The patient denies any recent infections, fever, or joint swelling, but notes occasional fatigue. On examination, there is reduced pinprick sensation in a glove-and-stocking distribution. Vibration sense is decreased in the lower extremities. Deep tendon reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Laboratory studies show elevated fasting blood glucose and HbA1c levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this patient's symptoms?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of fatigue, joint pain, and occasional fevers for the past 3 months. He mentions that his knees and wrists are particularly affected, with morning stiffness lasting about an hour. On further questioning, he also reports easy bruising and occasional nosebleeds. His medical history is unremarkable. On examination, he appears pale, and there is mild hepatosplenomegaly. Laboratory tests reveal pancytopenia with a hemoglobin level of 9 g/dL, platelet count of 80,000/mm^3, and a white blood cell count of 3,000/mm^3. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in the management of this patient?

options.C: Nucleic acid amplification test for parvovirus B19

question: A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of generalized fatigue, night sweats, and unintentional weight loss. He also reports intermittent fevers and a persistent, non-tender swelling of the lymph nodes in his neck and both axillae. He denies any recent infections, and his past medical history is unremarkable. He is a non-smoker and consumes alcohol occasionally. He recently noticed some mild joint pain but attributes it to increased physical activity. On examination, he is afebrile with a pulse of 82/min, blood pressure of 130/80 mmHg, and respirations at 18/min. Cervical and axillary lymphadenopathy is noted, with the largest node measuring 2 cm in diameter. Laboratory studies show a normocytic anemia and elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). A chest x-ray reveals mediastinal widening. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old male presents to the clinic with diffuse body aches and fatigue that started two weeks ago. He reports a recent upper respiratory tract infection that resolved on its own a week ago. He denies any significant past medical history and is not on any medications. He has a temperature of 37.2°C (98.9°F), blood pressure is 122/76 mmHg, pulse is 78/min, and respirations are 16/min. On physical examination, he appears tired but in no acute distress. Musculoskeletal examination reveals generalized tenderness, especially around the shoulders and thighs, but no swelling or erythema is noted. Laboratory findings show normal complete blood count, but there is a slight elevation in erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP). Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.B: Order rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies
options.C: Refer to rheumatology for further evaluation

question: A 60-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of chronic neck pain and tingling in his arms over the past six months. He describes the pain as dull and persistent, occasionally radiating to his shoulders and upper back. He denies any recent trauma or injury to the area. The patient has a history of hypertension, which is well-controlled with medication, and he does not smoke or drink alcohol. On physical examination, there is limited neck range of motion, and Spurling's test is positive. Neurological examination reveals diminished sensation in a glove-like distribution on both hands and reduced strength in the biceps bilaterally. Laboratory studies show a normal complete blood count and thyroid function tests. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP) are slightly elevated. Cervical spine X-rays show spondylotic changes with narrowing of the C5-C6 intervertebral disc space. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 34-year-old man presents to his primary care clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash. He reports a recent hiking trip in the northeastern United States, where he recalls removing a tick from his arm about two weeks ago. Since then, he has experienced fatigue, intermittent fevers, and migratory arthritis affecting his knees and wrists. On examination, a characteristic erythematous rash with central clearing is noted on his back. Laboratory studies show mild leukocytosis, elevated ESR, and normal rheumatoid factor levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 39-year-old man presents to his primary care physician with complaints of widespread musculoskeletal pain, fatigue, and difficulty sleeping for the last six months. He describes the pain as a constant dull ache affecting his muscles and joints, and notes that it has been accompanied by significant fatigue, despite sufficient sleep at night. His medical history is significant for a remote back injury but is otherwise unremarkable. He works in a sedentary job and denies recent trauma or changes in his activity level. On physical examination, there are tender points in multiple muscle groups, but no joint swelling or erythema. Laboratory studies reveal normal inflammatory markers and a negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the rheumatology clinic with a 2-year history of swelling and pain in his hands and feet. He reports morning stiffness lasting more than an hour, improving with movement throughout the day. His medical history is significant for hypertension. Physical examination reveals symmetrical swelling and tenderness in the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Laboratory tests show positive rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibodies. The patient's erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) is elevated. An X-ray of the hands shows joint space narrowing and marginal erosions. What is the most appropriate treatment to initiate in this patient?

A 55-year-old man visits the clinic due to a 3-month history of difficulty walking and frequent tripping, primarily with his left foot. He reports that he has to frequently 'lift' his left foot higher to avoid dragging it on the ground. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 10 years ago. The patient admits to occasional numbness in the feet but denies any recent trauma. On physical examination, he has a left foot drop and mild atrophy of the left tibialis anterior muscle. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is reduced in a stocking distribution below the knees bilaterally. Laboratory tests reveal a hemoglobin A1c of 8.4%. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of this patient's symptoms?

A 54-year-old male presents to the clinic with a 6-month history of recurrent sinusitis and nasal congestion. He also reports shortness of breath and a non-productive cough that has progressively worsened over the past few months. Additionally, he has experienced unintentional weight loss and fatigue. He denies any previous history of asthma or allergies. Past medical history is significant for hypertension controlled with medication. On examination, there is tenderness over the maxillary sinuses and decreased breath sounds with occasional crackles in the lung fields. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated serum creatinine level and positive c-ANCA. A chest X-ray shows bilateral pulmonary infiltrates. A renal biopsy is performed and shows necrotizing granulomas. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 50-year-old man visits the dermatology clinic due to progressive tightening of the skin on his fingers and frequent episodes of color changes in his fingers triggered by cold exposure. He describes episodes where his fingers turn white, then blue, and finally red and painful as they warm up. He denies any joint swelling but mentions occasional joint pain and fatigue. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no regular medications. On physical examination, sclerodactyly is noted, and there are telangiectasias on his face. Laboratory tests show a positive antinuclear antibody (ANA) with a titer of 1:160 and an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 17-year-old male presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that began a week ago. He reports that the pain started in his knees and has since moved to his ankles, elbows, and wrists. He recalls having a sore throat two weeks prior to the onset of these symptoms but did not seek medical attention at that time. On physical examination, he appears uncomfortable, with warm, swollen, and tender joints. His temperature is 38.2°C (100.8°F). Laboratory tests reveal elevated C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Anti-streptolysin O titer is also elevated. What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 42-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling that has been worsening over the past three months. He reports asymmetric pain in his right knee and left ankle, along with stiffness that lasts less than 30 minutes in the morning. Additionally, he has developed red, scaly patches on his elbows and knees, which he attributes to dry skin. He decides to wear long sleeves to cover these patches, which have been present for several years but are now more extensive. The patient denies fever, weight loss, or recent infections. Family history is significant for psoriasis in his mother. Physical examination reveals swelling and tenderness of the right knee and left ankle, along with well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scales on the extensor surfaces. Laboratory tests show elevated C-reactive protein (CRP) and erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR). What is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old male comes to the clinic with acute onset of right knee pain and swelling after participating in a weekend soccer match. He describes the pain as severe and notes that it worsens with weight-bearing. On examination, the right knee is warm, swollen, and tender to touch with a decreased range of motion. The patient denies any history of trauma or previous joint issues. He has a mild fever, and laboratory tests show elevated white blood cell count and ESR. An x-ray of the knee shows soft tissue swelling but no fractures. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 17-year-old male is brought to the clinic by his mother because of joint pain. He reports experiencing sore throat two weeks ago that resolved with over-the-counter medications. Since then, he has had swelling and pain in his knees, ankles, and wrists, and the pain seems to move from one joint to another. He denies any trauma or previous similar episodes. On examination, he is afebrile but appears uncomfortable due to joint pain. There is erythema and swelling in the affected joints, with no deformities noted. Laboratory studies show an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate and C-reactive protein. A rapid strep test performed today is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the clinic with complaints of joint pain and a rash. He recalls being bitten by a tick two weeks ago during a hiking trip. He initially experienced flu-like symptoms but now reports significant pain in his knees and ankles, along with a characteristic rash that appears as a circular, red area with central clearing on his upper arms. He denies any history of previous joint problems. On examination, there is mild swelling of the affected joints, and the rash is consistent with erythema migrans. Blood tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers but negative rheumatoid factor. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with a 3-month history of numbness and tingling in his feet and hands. He describes the sensation as 'pins and needles' and notes it is worse at night. He denies any recent trauma or significant physical activity changes. His medical history is significant for type 2 diabetes mellitus, which is poorly controlled, and hypertension. He drinks alcohol socially and has a 20 pack-year smoking history. His medications include metformin, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. On examination, he has decreased sensation to light touch and vibration in the distal extremities. Reflexes are 1+ in the ankles. Laboratory studies reveal a hemoglobin A1c of 9.2%. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 32-year-old male presents to the clinic with acute right knee pain and swelling that began after playing a game of basketball two days ago. He reports that the knee was initially slightly painful during the game but became significantly more swollen and painful overnight. He denies any fever, chills, or other joint involvement. His medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, the right knee is warm, swollen, and tender with a limited range of motion. There is no erythema or rash on the joint or surrounding skin. An x-ray of the knee shows no fractures or dislocations. Laboratory results show normal white blood cell count and elevated C-reactive protein. Which of the following is the next best step in management?

options.B: Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis

question: A 48-year-old man presents to the emergency department with left wrist pain after falling on an outstretched hand while jogging. He describes the pain as sharp and localized to the wrist, with significant swelling developing immediately after the fall. He denies any previous similar injuries or chronic joint issues. His medical history is notable for well-controlled hypertension and he takes lisinopril. On examination, there is tenderness over the distal radius and reduced range of motion due to pain. An X-ray of the wrist is performed. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

A 45-year-old man comes to the clinic due to multiple subcutaneous nodules on his elbows and forearms that have developed over the past few months. He also reports experiencing low-grade fevers and fatigue. He denies any recent infections, trauma, or significant weight changes. His medical history includes hypertension, for which he is on lisinopril. On examination, there are several firm, non-tender nodules on the extensor surfaces of his arms. Laboratory tests reveal an elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR), but normal renal and liver function tests. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 54-year-old male presents to his physician with complaints of chronic neck pain and intermittent tingling in his right arm and fingers. He states that the symptoms have been gradually worsening over the past six months. He works as an office manager and spends long hours at a desk. On examination, there is a limited range of motion in the cervical spine with tenderness along the paraspinal muscles. Spurling's maneuver reproduces his arm symptoms. Neurological examination shows decreased deep tendon reflexes in the biceps and diminished sensation in the thumb and index finger of the right hand. An MRI of the cervical spine shows disc osteophyte complexes at the C5-C6 level with mild spinal canal narrowing. Laboratory tests show a normal complete blood count and normal inflammatory markers. Based on the information provided, which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 28-year-old male presents with painful red nodules on his shins and joint pain in his knees and ankles. He reports feeling fatigued, and his symptoms have developed over the last two weeks. He denies any recent infections or changes in medications. Laboratory tests reveal elevated inflammatory markers, but rheumatoid factor is negative. A chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to the neurology clinic with complaints of progressive difficulty walking over the past several months. He describes a sensation of weakness in his legs, which he initially noticed during his morning jogs. He has now started using a cane for support. He denies any recent trauma but mentions occasional numbness and tingling in his lower extremities. He also complains of generalized fatigue. On physical examination, muscle strength in the lower extremities is 3/5 bilaterally, deep tendon reflexes are brisk, and there is bilateral ankle clonus. Babinski sign is present bilaterally. Laboratory tests show elevated creatine kinase levels. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 30-year-old man presents to the clinic with joint pain and swelling in both knees, ankles, and wrists for the past week. He also reports fever, fatigue, and a rash over his trunk and extremities. He returned from a trip to the Caribbean two weeks ago. He denies any previous history of joint problems or similar symptoms. Physical examination reveals a maculopapular rash and swelling with tenderness of the involved joints. Laboratory tests show leukopenia and thrombocytopenia. C-reactive protein is elevated, but rheumatoid factor is negative. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

A 52-year-old male presents to his primary care physician with complaints of skin tightening affecting his fingers and occasional episodes of hand whitening in response to cold exposure. He also mentions experiencing joint pain and fatigue over the past few months. On examination, the skin over his fingers appears shiny and taut, and there is evidence of pitting scars on the fingertips. His medical history reveals occasional heartburn and difficulty swallowing. Laboratory investigations demonstrate a positive ANA with a speckled pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
